name: Business Continuity
overview: Business continuity planning ensures critical operations can be sustained or rapidly restored following disruptions such as natural disasters, cyberattacks, equipment failures, or workforce impacts.
indicators_of_risk: ["No documented continuity plan or outdated content","Critical functions dependent on single points of failure","Backups or redundancies untested or nonfunctional","No prioritization of essential functions or recovery sequences","Vendors and supply chains not included in continuity planning"]
common_failures: ["Plans created as paperwork but never exercised","Leadership unaware of continuity priorities","No alternative work locations or remote capabilities","Overly optimistic assumptions about staff availability or supply resilience","Failure to integrate facilities, IT, and operations into unified planning"]
assessment_questions: ["What are the organization’s essential functions and maximum acceptable downtimes?","How quickly can critical operations be restored after a disruption?","Are backups, redundancies, and emergency resources tested regularly?","Does the continuity plan involve external partners and suppliers?","How are lessons from past incidents incorporated into updates?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Identify essential functions and establish prioritized recovery sequences.","Develop redundant systems, alternate locations, and remote capabilities.","Conduct table-top and functional continuity exercises regularly.","Integrate continuity planning with incident response and emergency management.","Coordinate with key vendors, utilities, and partners to ensure continuity of supply."]
standards_references: ["ISO 22301 – Business Continuity Management Systems","NFPA 1600 – Continuity, Emergency, and Crisis Management","FEMA continuity planning guidance (COOP/COG concepts)"]
psa_notes: Ask when the continuity plan was last exercised. If the answer is “never,” the plan is untested and likely unworkable in a real disruption.

name: Emergency Communications
overview: Emergency communications enable timely notification and coordination during incidents through systems such as mass notification, radios, PA systems, alerts, and secure internal messaging platforms.
indicators_of_risk: ["Staff unaware of how they will be notified during emergencies","No redundant communication paths (radio, PA, digital alerts)","Delayed or inconsistent dissemination of critical information","Messages unclear, jargon-heavy, or not scenario-specific","Systems dependent on single points of failure (power, network, staff)"]
common_failures: ["Reliance on only one communication method (email, phone trees, or PA)","No testing or drills involving the communication system","Messages not pre-scripted, causing confusion under stress","Insufficient reach to visitors, contractors, or remote workers","Emergency communications controlled by untrained staff"]
assessment_questions: ["How will staff be notified during different emergency scenarios?","What redundancy exists for communication failures?","Who is authorized to send emergency messages?","Are templates or pre-scripted messages prepared?","How are communications tested and evaluated?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Implement a multi-channel notification strategy (PA, SMS, radio, digital alerts).","Establish clear authority for initiating emergency communications.","Prepare scenario-based message templates for common hazards.","Test communication systems during drills and exercises.","Ensure communications reach all populations in the facility."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 72 – Emergency Communications Systems","CISA guidance on emergency messaging and alerting"]
psa_notes: Ask multiple staff members how they would be notified in emergencies. If answers differ, communications will fail when needed most.

name: Ballistic Barriers
overview: Ballistic barriers are engineered components—glazing, panels, walls, and protective furniture—rated to stop specific calibers or threat levels. They protect staff in reception, guard posts, and other exposed positions.
indicators_of_risk: ["Reception desks or guard stations directly exposed to public approach","Ballistic materials installed but with no rating documentation","Gaps between panels creating bullet-through paths","Ballistic glazing installed in non-ballistic frames","Furniture or decorative elements defeating barrier coverage"]
common_failures: ["Using “bullet-resistant” marketing terms without verified ratings","Failing to protect seams, edges, and joints between panels","Installing barriers only at eye-level and ignoring lower/upper exposure zones","Relying on ballistic glazing without matching wall reinforcement","Not training staff on how to use barriers and maintain standoff"]
assessment_questions: ["What ballistic rating (UL 752, NIJ, etc.) applies to your barriers?","Do barriers provide continuous protection (no open seams)?","Are reception or guard staff trained on barrier use and positioning?","Were barriers installed by certified personnel?","Do barriers align with identified threat levels in the risk assessment?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Install UL- or NIJ-rated barriers appropriate for the expected threat level.","Ensure seams, joints, and transitions are hardened to prevent bypass.","Use ballistic-rated frames with ballistic glazing.","Conduct training on standoff and using cover effectively.","Integrate barriers into the overall architectural and protective design."]
standards_references: ["UL 752 – ballistic resistance standards","NIJ ballistic protection standards","DoD and CISA protective design references"]
psa_notes: Inspect joints and edges carefully—these are the most common failure points. A barrier is only as strong as its weakest seam.

name: Muster Points / Rally Areas
overview: Muster Points / Rally Areas ensure occupants have safe, clearly identified locations to assemble during evacuations or emergency movements. Proper placement, signage, access routes, and accountability procedures are essential for life safety.
indicators_of_risk: ["Rally areas located too close to the building or hazard zones","No signage or unclear evacuation routes leading to the muster point","Areas not sized appropriately for expected occupant volume","Obstructions or poor lighting along evacuation paths","Staff unable to identify or explain designated muster points"]
common_failures: ["Selecting rally areas based on convenience rather than safety standoff","No secondary or tertiary muster locations for alternate scenarios","Failure to include people with disabilities in muster planning","Using parking lots with active traffic as assembly areas"]
assessment_questions: ["Where are the primary and alternate muster points located?","Are muster locations outside known blast, debris, or vehicle paths?","Is signage present and visible from evacuation routes?","Are staff trained and drilled on evacuation and assembly procedures?","How are personnel accounted for once assembled?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Select muster points with adequate distance and protection from facility hazards.","Provide multiple rally areas to support scenario-based evacuations.","Install clear, reflective evacuation and assembly signage.","Ensure unobstructed, ADA-compliant paths leading to muster points.","Conduct regular evacuation drills including accountability checks."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 101 Life Safety Code","OSHA 29 CFR 1910.38 (Emergency Action Plans)"]
psa_notes: PSAs should physically walk evacuation paths and confirm muster locations are safe, accessible, and realistically usable under stress conditions.

name: Security Procedures
overview: Security procedures are the step-by-step instructions for how staff implement security policies during routine operations and emergencies. They convert broad policy statements into specific, repeatable actions.
indicators_of_risk: ["Staff give conflicting answers about what to do in common security scenarios","Procedures exist only in long policy documents, not in usable job aids or checklists","Procedures treat all times and conditions the same, ignoring after-hours or special events","No crosswalk between procedures and actual technology capabilities (e.g., cameras, access control)","Procedures never referenced during drills or incident reviews"]
common_failures: ["Procedures written once and never updated as systems, layouts, or threats change","Procedures overly complex or written in legalistic language staff do not use","Key procedures dependent on a single role or person being present","Gaps between procedures for day shift and night/weekend operations"]
assessment_questions: ["Do you maintain written procedures for routine security tasks and common incidents?","How do staff access procedures quickly during an event?","Do you test and refine procedures through drills and after-action reviews?","Are procedures aligned with your actual staffing, equipment, and layout?","Who owns the process of updating and approving procedure changes?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Translate policies into concise, role-based procedures and quick-reference guides.","Validate procedures through walkthroughs, drills, and tabletop exercises.","Assign clear ownership for procedure maintenance and updates.","Ensure procedures incorporate the capabilities and limitations of actual security systems.","Simplify procedures where possible and prioritize clarity over completeness under stress."]
standards_references: ["CISA physical security planning guidance","NFPA 730 – premises security recommendations for procedures","ISO/management-system style guidance on documented procedures (adapted to physical security)"]
psa_notes: Ask staff to describe, step by step, what they would do in a scenario. Their answer is the real procedure—policies and binders are only meaningful if they match that behavior.

name: Impact-Resistant Glazing
overview: Impact-resistant glazing is engineered to withstand forced entry, windborne debris, and certain ballistic or blast events depending on rating. It provides true delay and hazard reduction compared to standard glass with film.
indicators_of_risk: ["Critical entry points using standard tempered or plate glass","No documentation of glazing rating (ASTM, UL, or local building code)","Frames or muntins not reinforced, undermining glazing strength","Impact glazing installed only on select panes leaving bypass routes","Glazing not tested or inspected after major storms or impacts"]
common_failures: ["Assuming code-compliant hurricane glazing equals forced-entry glazing","Installing strong glass in weak frames that fail first","Failing to integrate glazing design with locks, doors, and surrounding walls","Using outdated or uncertified products in high-risk facilities","Not training staff about glazing limitations and expected delay times"]
assessment_questions: ["What rating does your glazing carry and what threat scenario was it selected for?","Are the surrounding frames reinforced consistent with the glazing?","Do access points have continuous hardening across all adjacent panes?","How often is glazing inspected, especially after weather events?","Is glazing included in your forced-entry and storm hardening strategy?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Use certified forced-entry or impact-rated glazing for critical doors and windows.","Ensure frame assemblies match the glazing strength and rating.","Apply hardening consistently, eliminating adjacent bypass panes.","Inspect glazing after storms, impacts, or renovation vibration.","Integrate glazing upgrades with door hardware and structural enhancements."]
standards_references: ["ASTM F1233 – forced-entry and ballistic resistant glazing","ASTM E1886/E1996 – impact-resistant glazing standards","UL 972 – burglary-resistant glazing"]
psa_notes: Glazing is only as strong as the frame. Ask for both the glazing certificate AND the frame specifications.

name: Key Cabinets
overview: Key cabinets provide secure storage and accountability for mechanical keys, fobs, and other physical access devices. They are essential when electronic access control is mixed with traditional keyed systems.
indicators_of_risk: ["Keys stored in unlocked drawers or hung openly near doors","No tracking of who removed keys and when","Master keys mixed with routine keys, increasing risk of compromise","Key cabinet not located in an access-controlled area","Lost keys not documented or rekeying not performed"]
common_failures: ["Assigning shared keys with no individual accountability","Allowing contractors to hold keys long-term without tracking","Failing to separate master keys, sub-master keys, and general keys","Key cabinets left unlocked for convenience","No reconciliation of key inventory with physical locks"]
assessment_questions: ["Where are keys stored and how is access to the cabinet controlled?","How do you track key issuance and return?","Do you maintain a master key matrix and update it after changes?","What happens when keys are lost or unreturned?","Are high-security or restricted keys stored separately?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Use lockable, access-controlled key cabinets with audit trails.","Maintain a detailed, updated key control matrix.","Enforce check-in/check-out procedures for all keys.","Separate master and high-security keys from general keys.","Implement rekeying procedures for lost or compromised keys."]
standards_references: ["ASIS physical key control guidelines","NFPA 730 key management principles"]
psa_notes: Ask to randomly pull the key log. If entries are blank or inconsistent, the key cabinet is functionally unsecured.

name: Boundary Demarcation
overview: Boundary demarcation establishes clear visual or physical cues defining where public space ends and controlled facility space begins. It serves as deterrence, aids in wayfinding, and enhances legal enforceability of restricted areas.
indicators_of_risk: ["Ambiguous property lines leading to unintentional trespass","Lack of visual cues distinguishing public vs. private zones","Conflicting or missing markings at transitions between areas","Unauthorized vehicles or pedestrians entering due to unclear boundaries","Inconsistent signage or fencing causing confusion"]
common_failures: ["Relying on natural features (curbs, grass) instead of proper demarcation","Using mixed or outdated signage styles that confuse visitors","Failing to align boundaries with security patrol routes or camera views","Allowing vegetation to conceal boundary markers or signage"]
assessment_questions: ["How does the facility visually communicate its property lines?","Are restricted boundaries consistently marked and maintained?","Are visitors able to easily identify where to enter?","Is boundary demarcation aligned with camera and lighting coverage?","Do security staff know where the legal property line actually is?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Use consistent fencing, curbing, landscaping, and signage to communicate boundaries.","Mark transitions with lighting, pavement markings, or gate structures.","Ensure vegetation maintenance supports visibility of markers.","Align boundary features with surveillance and patrol patterns.","Create a boundary demarcation plan and map for enforcement."]
standards_references: ["CPTED territorial reinforcement principles","CISA perimeter security design guidance"]
psa_notes: If you have to ask “where does the property actually start?” the demarcation is insufficient. Clear boundaries are foundational to all other perimeter measures.

name: Landscaping Control
overview: Landscaping control manages vegetation, planters, berms, and site furnishings so they support security objectives instead of creating hiding places, climb aids, or vehicle concealment.
indicators_of_risk: ["Dense shrubs or trees directly adjacent to entries, windows, or walkways creating concealment","Vegetation used as ad hoc screening for parking or storage areas that also obscures observation","Trees, walls, or planters close enough to fences or roofs to serve as ladders or launch points","Landscaping blocking sightlines between staffed positions and key approach routes","Overgrown vegetation along fence lines, clear zones, or around lighting and cameras"]
common_failures: ["Landscaping decisions driven solely by aesthetics or maintenance cost with no security input","Plantings allowed to mature beyond original design height, eliminating natural surveillance","Using raised planters or berms that inadvertently shield vehicles from view near buildings","Not coordinating landscaping with CPTED principles, lighting, and camera placement","Failing to include landscaping checks in routine patrols and inspection programs"]
assessment_questions: ["Have landscaping standards or guidelines been developed that include security considerations?","Do planting plans limit concealment near doors, windows, and pedestrian paths?","Are trees and large shrubs monitored so they do not become climb aids or observation blockers?","Is landscaping reviewed after major changes to lighting, cameras, or fencing?","Who is responsible for enforcing pruning and removal when security concerns arise?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Adopt CPTED-informed landscaping standards specifying maximum heights and setbacks.","Maintain low plantings near entrances and windows to preserve sightlines.","Avoid placing climbable trees or structures next to fences, walls, or roofs.","Integrate landscaping design reviews into security, lighting, and camera projects.","Include landscaping conditions in routine security inspections and corrective work orders."]
standards_references: ["CPTED (Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design) guidance on landscaping and visibility","CISA soft target and crowded places security resources with site design considerations"]
psa_notes: If you can disappear into the bushes within a few steps of the door, so can an attacker. PSAs should challenge “privacy” plantings that undermine basic visibility and control.

name: Patrol Routes
overview: Patrol routes define how security personnel or staff move through the facility and grounds to deter misconduct, detect anomalies, and respond quickly. Effective patrols are purposeful, documented, and risk-driven—not random wandering.
indicators_of_risk: ["No documented patrol routes or schedules—guards “just walk around”","Large areas of the facility rarely or never patrolled","Predictable, easily observed patrol patterns","Patrols focused on low-risk interior areas while high-risk exteriors are ignored","No method to document patrol completion, findings, or anomalies"]
common_failures: ["Patrol routes inherited from legacy operations and never re-evaluated","Patrols reduced or skipped during busy periods without risk assessment","Over-reliance on vehicle patrols that miss interior or pedestrian-scale issues","No integration between patrol observations and incident reporting or work orders","Patrol routes not updated after facility expansions or layout changes"]
assessment_questions: ["Are patrol routes documented, and how were they designed?","How often are high-risk areas (perimeter, parking, mechanical rooms) checked?","Do patrols vary timing and direction to avoid predictability?","How do guards record and report observations from patrols?","When was the last time patrol routes were reviewed or adjusted?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Design patrol routes based on risk assessment, coverage gaps, and incident history.","Ensure patrols cover key interior and exterior areas, including blind spots.","Vary patrol timing and direction to reduce predictability.","Implement simple documentation methods (tour systems, logs, or apps) tied to follow-up actions.","Review and update patrol routes after renovations, major incidents, or pattern changes."]
standards_references: ["ASIS Guidelines for Security Officer Patrols","CPTED and CISA guidance on natural surveillance and security presence"]
psa_notes: Ask guards to describe their routes and show you where they actually go. The difference between the post orders and the reality is where risk lives.

name: Vehicle Access Control Points
overview: Vehicle Access Control Points (VACPs) control and screen vehicular entry into secured facilities. They rely on barriers, signage, procedures, guard verification, and sensor systems.
indicators_of_risk: ["Vehicles permitted entry without credential or verification","No physical barrier to prevent ramming or unauthorized drive-throughs","Confusing signage leading to improper lane usage","Guards exposed with no protective booths or standoff","No camera coverage of license plates or driver faces"]
common_failures: ["Allowing habitual “wave-throughs” without checks","Improper lane design causing traffic mixing between visitors and staff","No denial lane or exit lane design for denied vehicles","Poor lighting reducing visibility of occupants and plates","Failure to document or enforce screening SOPs"]
assessment_questions: ["What is the screening process for staff, visitors, and vendors?","Are barriers rated and properly integrated with guard procedures?","Do guards have protected positions and clear lines of sight?","How is traffic flow managed during peak periods?","Are VACPs monitored by cameras and access logs?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Implement tiered screening based on vehicle type and risk.","Provide guard booths, standoff distance, and escape lanes.","Install proper traffic control devices and logical lane separation.","Enhance lighting and camera coverage for driver and plate identification.","Document and enforce screening procedures with training and refreshers."]
standards_references: ["DoD/USACE Access Control Point standards (adapted where appropriate)","CISA guidance for vehicle screening and controlled points"]
psa_notes: Observe peak-time operations—shortcuts and deviations from SOPs reveal the true state of VACP discipline.

name: Keypads / PIN Entry
overview: Keypads and PIN entry systems authenticate individuals using numeric codes to control access to secured areas. They are common in low-to-medium risk environments and require strong management of codes, user discipline, and tamper controls.
indicators_of_risk: ["Codes shared among multiple employees","Visible wear patterns on frequently pressed digits","PINs not changed after staff turnover","Keypad located in public or visible areas without shielding","No lockout or alarm after repeated failed attempts"]
common_failures: ["Using a single shared PIN for all personnel","Not updating PIN codes regularly or after employee departures","Keypads installed where passersby can shoulder-surf entry codes","Failure to configure tamper alarms or lockout timers","No audit trail of who accessed the area and when"]
assessment_questions: ["How often are PIN codes changed?","Are PINs assigned individually or shared among groups?","Does the keypad have a lockout after failed attempts?","Is the keypad visible from public or visitor areas?","Are PIN entry logs or access attempts monitored?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Assign individual PINs tied to personnel rather than shared group codes.","Implement quarterly PIN changes and immediate updates after staff separation.","Add keypad shields or reposition devices to prevent PIN observation.","Enable lockout, tamper alarms, and monitoring of failed attempts.","Transition to card/PIN dual-factor authentication for higher-risk areas."]
standards_references: ["ISC Physical Access Control Standards","ASIS Access Control Guidelines"]
psa_notes: Check for worn buttons and observe whether personnel hide PIN entry. If everyone uses one code, accountability is nonexistent.

name: Emergency Guides / Flip Charts
overview: Emergency guides and flip charts provide quick-reference instructions for staff during emergencies, summarizing evacuation, lockdown, medical response, and communication procedures in concise, accessible formats.
indicators_of_risk: ["Flip charts outdated or inconsistent with current emergency plans","Guides not posted in classrooms, offices, or common areas","Digital versions not accessible during network outages","Employees unaware guides exist or unsure how to interpret them","No multilingual or ADA-accessible versions where needed"]
common_failures: ["Treating guides as compliance documents instead of operational tools","Creating overly complex guides difficult to follow during stress","Failing to update guides after layout or procedural changes","Not placing guides in areas where they are actually needed","Lack of standardization across buildings or departments"]
assessment_questions: ["Where are emergency guides posted and how often are they updated?","Do guides reflect the current hazard plan and facility layout?","Are guides available in formats accessible to all occupants?","Do staff know where to find and how to interpret these guides?","Are guides used during drills and training sessions?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Standardize emergency guide content and design across the facility.","Ensure guides are posted at all workstations and occupant spaces.","Update guides after procedural or layout changes.","Provide multilingual and accessible formats as required.","Reference guides during training and drills to reinforce use."]
standards_references: ["OSHA emergency planning communication standards","FEMA and CISA emergency communication best practices"]
psa_notes: Flip charts are only useful if they’re visible, current, and understood. Outdated or ignored guides can create more confusion than clarity.

name: Rekeying Procedures
overview: Rekeying procedures outline how facilities respond when keys are lost, stolen, or compromised. Effective rekeying reduces unauthorized access risks.
indicators_of_risk: ["Lost keys not triggering any formal action or investigation","No inventory of which locks each key opens","Master key loss treated as minor rather than urgent security issue","Rekeying decisions based on cost rather than risk","Contractors or staff retaining keys long after their access should end"]
common_failures: ["Delays in rekeying due to cost or convenience concerns","No documentation of rekeying events, leaving unclear audit trails","Partial rekeying that leaves vulnerable bypass paths","Failure to update electronic access control roles after mechanical rekeying"]
assessment_questions: ["What events trigger a rekeying?","How long does it typically take to complete a rekey after a key is lost?","Do you document which locks and keys are affected?","Who authorizes rekeying, and how is risk considered in the decision?","Are access control systems updated in parallel with mechanical changes?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Define triggers for rekeying and document them in key control policies.","Prioritize rapid rekeying for master or high-risk keys.","Maintain detailed records of all rekeying activities.","Synchronize mechanical rekeying with updates to electronic systems.","Conduct periodic audits to verify that rekeying policies are followed."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 730 – guidance on key control and rekeying","CISA access control and lock management best practices"]
psa_notes: Master key loss is a major event. PSAs should ask to review recent rekeying history and ensure timelines align with security expectations.

name: Camera Coverage / Line of Sight
overview: Camera coverage determines how effectively video surveillance can detect events and support investigations. Proper line-of-sight and overlapping coverage are essential.
indicators_of_risk: ["Primary entrances not covered by cameras","Obstructed camera views caused by signage, decor, or equipment","Wide open areas covered by a single camera at excessive distance","No overlapping coverage for critical points","Cameras facing windows causing silhouette or glare"]
common_failures: ["Placement based on convenience (aesthetics or electrician routes)","Failure to adjust cameras as layouts change","Coverage gaps around blind corners and approach routes","Camera height too high to capture usable imagery"]
assessment_questions: ["How was camera placement decided originally?","Do you have documented coverage maps or audits?","Where are your most important blind spots?","Do cameras capture faces at entry points?","How often is camera alignment checked?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Conduct a coverage audit for priority entrances and high-risk spaces.","Reposition or add cameras to eliminate blind spots.","Lower or angle cameras for identification-quality images.","Document and maintain camera coverage plans."]
standards_references: ["IEC 62676 – video surveillance planning","CISA camera placement recommendations"]
psa_notes: A single missing angle can undermine the entire system. PSAs should always assess approach routes and chokepoints.

name: Access-Restricted Areas
overview: Access-restricted areas contain sensitive assets, records, systems, or operations requiring controlled entry. They rely on defined authorization levels, access controls, monitoring, and operational discipline.
indicators_of_risk: ["Doors to restricted rooms left unsecured or propped open","Staff unclear about who is authorized to enter certain areas","Sensitive spaces lacking surveillance or door monitoring","Shared keys or access cards used by multiple employees","Visitors observed entering restricted zones without escorts"]
common_failures: ["Using “access restricted” signage without actual controls in place","Permitting unauthorized staff into secure areas for convenience","Failing to maintain access lists or review them regularly","No accountability for misuse of credentials or tailgating","Storing sensitive items in non-restricted rooms due to overflow"]
assessment_questions: ["What criteria define restricted areas in this facility?","How is authorization granted, reviewed, and revoked?","Are these spaces monitored with cameras or sensors?","How often are access lists/audit logs reviewed?","Do staff understand their responsibilities within restricted zones?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Define restricted areas based on asset criticality and operational needs.","Implement and enforce role-based access controls.","Add door monitoring, cameras, or audit logging to sensitive rooms.","Review access lists quarterly and after staffing changes.","Train staff to challenge unauthorized presence in restricted areas."]
standards_references: ["ISC Physical Access Control guidance","ASIS General Security Risk Standards"]
psa_notes: Restricted areas often erode over time—ask for access list reviews and observe whether the door is actually secured in practice.

name: Security Officer Training
overview: Security officer training ensures guards understand site-specific risks, procedures, and systems. Contract or in-house officers are often the first to observe and respond to suspicious activity or incidents.
indicators_of_risk: ["Officers unfamiliar with emergency procedures or can only describe “call 911” as a response","Guards unaware of camera coverage, alarm points, or key risk areas","Training focused only on company policies, not site-specific expectations","New officers deployed solo with minimal onboarding at the site","No documentation of completed training or ongoing refreshers"]
common_failures: ["Reliance on generic contract-guard training without local adaptation","High turnover with no structured method to maintain training continuity","Training delivered once at hire and never revisited","No integration between officer training and facility drills or incident reviews"]
assessment_questions: ["What training do security officers receive specifically for this facility?","How are they trained on emergency procedures, patrol routes, and post orders?","How do you verify that new or temporary officers are properly onboarded?","Do officers participate in drills, exercises, or after-action reviews?","How often is training refreshed or updated for existing officers?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Create a site-specific training module for all officers assigned to the facility.","Include tours of critical areas, system demonstrations, and scenario-based practice.","Require documentation and sign-off for completed training and refreshers.","Integrate officers fully into drills and debriefs as key stakeholders.","Coordinate closely with guard companies to enforce training standards in contracts."]
standards_references: ["ASIS Private Security Officer Selection and Training Guidelines","CISA protective security operations guidance","Any jurisdictional licensing or training mandates for security officers"]
psa_notes: Interview officers directly. Their understanding of the site and procedures often tells you more about real security posture than a stack of policies ever will.

name: Door Monitoring
overview: Door monitoring uses sensors to detect door open/door closed states, forced entry, propped doors, and unauthorized access patterns. It provides real-time situational awareness and is a critical component of electronic access control.
indicators_of_risk: ["No sensors on high-security or high-traffic doors","Propped doors not generating alarms or notifications","False alarms due to loose door hardware or poor alignment","Sensors bypassed with tape or magnets","Monitoring software not reviewed or alarmed events ignored"]
common_failures: ["Failing to configure door-held-open timers","Not integrating sensors with alarm monitoring or VMS","Supervisors ignoring propped door alarms due to frequency","Sensor placement that allows easy defeat or tampering"]
assessment_questions: ["Which doors have monitoring sensors installed?","Do propped or forced doors trigger alarms?","Are alarms monitored 24/7 or logged?","Are sensors aligned and tested regularly?","Is there a process for investigating repeated alarms?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Install monitoring sensors on all critical or perimeter doors.","Set appropriate door-held-open timers and alarm thresholds.","Integrate door alarms with VMS for automatic video pull-up.","Secure sensor wiring and protect components from tampering.","Investigate recurring alarms to fix underlying issues."]
standards_references: ["ISC Access Control Standards","ASIS Security System Monitoring Guidelines"]
psa_notes: Propped doors represent one of the highest-risk access control failures. Observe actual staff behavior — not just reported policy.

name: Governance & Oversight
overview: Governance and oversight define who owns the security program, how decisions are made, and how performance is monitored. Strong governance turns ad hoc security measures into a coordinated, accountable system.
indicators_of_risk: ["No designated security lead or committee with decision authority","Security decisions made reactively after incidents rather than strategically","Policies and procedures created but not enforced or reviewed","No defined reporting line for security issues to senior leadership or the board","Budgets and projects not linked to documented risk or performance measures"]
common_failures: ["Assuming security is “everyone’s responsibility” without clear ownership","Fragmented responsibilities across departments with no single coordinating body","Lack of regular security reporting to leadership (metrics, incidents, progress)","No formal process for approving, prioritizing, and tracking mitigation projects","Security left to vendors or contractors with minimal internal oversight"]
assessment_questions: ["Who is ultimately accountable for physical security at this facility or organization?","Is there a standing security or safety committee, and how often does it meet?","What metrics or indicators are reported to leadership regarding security performance?","How are security priorities set and funded?","How are audits, assessments, and after-action reviews tracked through to completion?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Designate a security program owner with clear authority and reporting lines.","Establish a cross-functional security committee that meets regularly.","Define and track key performance indicators (incidents, completion of mitigations, training rates).","Align budgets and projects with the results of risk assessments and audits.","Document decision-making and follow-through for major security issues."]
standards_references: ["ASIS Security Management and Governance guidelines","CISA guidance on security program governance and risk management frameworks"]
psa_notes: If you cannot quickly identify who owns security and what their authority is, governance is weak. PSAs should push for clarity in ownership, metrics, and follow-through.

name: Secured Vestibules
overview: Secured vestibules create a controlled point of entry where visitors can be screened before they are allowed deeper into the facility. They are especially important in schools, healthcare, and public-facing government facilities.
indicators_of_risk: ["Public can walk directly from the exterior into corridors, offices, or classrooms without interaction at reception","Reception desk is located deep inside the building rather than at the primary entrance","Interior doors from vestibule to secure side are not access-controlled or are often left unsecured","No clear line of sight from staff to the vestibule and primary entry doors","Visitor management process is informal or bypassed during busy periods"]
common_failures: ["Vestibule design exists on paper but doors are routinely left unlocked for convenience","No physical separation between public lobby and student/patient/staff areas","Visitors are not consistently signed in, badged, or escorted beyond the vestibule","Architectural glass or walls do not provide adequate delay from forced entry","Staff cannot safely deny entry or retreat if behavior escalates at the vestibule"]
assessment_questions: ["Describe how visitors enter the building during normal operations. Where are they stopped first?","Is there ever a time when the public can reach classrooms, treatment areas, or offices without passing a controlled point?","How do you handle individuals who become agitated or combative during the check-in process?","Are vestibule doors and hardware routinely inspected to ensure proper function?","Is visitor management integrated with access control or done manually?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Reconfigure building entry sequence so all visitors pass through a staffed, controlled vestibule.","Install access control on interior doors from vestibule to secure areas, with remote release at reception.","Provide staff in the vestibule with clear procedures and training for denial of entry and emergency response.","Enhance glazing and door assemblies at the vestibule to provide delay against forced entry where appropriate.","Integrate visitor management systems with the access control environment where feasible."]
standards_references: ["CISA K-12 School Security Guide – entry and vestibule controls","FEMA 428 / 430 – guidance on building protection and entry hardening","NFPA 730 – premises security (public entry control)"]
psa_notes: PSAs should trace the visitor journey from parking lot to final destination. If a determined member of the public can get to high-value areas in seconds with little or no challenge, the vestibule concept is either missing or not effective in practice.

name: Mass Notification
overview: Mass notification systems provide rapid, facility-wide communication during emergencies. Their value depends on clarity, reach, redundancy, and how well staff understand when and how to activate them.
indicators_of_risk: ["Staff unsure how to activate alerts or what messages mean","Notifications depend on a single device or person","Audible alerts not heard in some areas of the facility","Multiple uncoordinated systems used inconsistently","No logging or after-action review of alert usage"]
common_failures: ["System relies on manual activation by a person who may not be present","Notifications do not reach outdoor or high-noise areas","Messages are unclear or too generic to be actionable","Mass notification system not integrated with emergency plans"]
assessment_questions: ["What emergency messages exist and when are they used?","How do staff activate the system, and who has that authority?","Do you have redundancy across audible, visual, and digital alerting?","When was the system last tested facility-wide?","Do alerts integrate with any automation or lockdown measures?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Create standardized alert messages aligned to emergency actions like lockdown, evacuation, or shelter.","Ensure alerting covers all occupied spaces, indoors and outdoors.","Train all staff on activation and message meaning.","Conduct regular tests with documented coverage checks.","Integrate alerting with emergency plans and response procedures."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 72 – Emergency Communications Systems","CISA guidance on emergency notification","UFC 4-021-01 Mass Notification Systems"]
psa_notes: PSAs should physically verify audibility/visibility of alerts in critical areas. In most facilities, gaps exist where staff simply cannot hear or see alerts.

name: Electronic Access Control
overview: Electronic Access Control (EAC) governs who can go where, when, and under what conditions. It is a primary control for keeping unauthorized people out of sensitive areas and providing an audit trail of access activity.
indicators_of_risk: ["Doors that do not latch, are propped open, or have tape/objects defeating hardware","Frequent tailgating at main entrances or sensitive areas","Temporary badges or contractor access not visibly differentiated from permanent staff","Panels, controllers, or power supplies located in unsecured spaces","No central monitoring of access events or alarms"]
common_failures: ["No routine review of access privileges for staff and contractors","Former employees or vendors retaining valid credentials","High-risk areas (server rooms, security rooms, pharmacies) treated like ordinary offices","Access control not integrated with intrusion or video monitoring","No documented process for lost, stolen, or compromised badges"]
assessment_questions: ["How often do you review and recertify access permissions for staff, contractors, and vendors?","What is the process when a badge is lost, stolen, or an employee leaves?","Which areas are considered high-risk, and do they require additional authentication or controls?","Are access events or alarms monitored in real time, and by whom?","Where are controllers and power supplies located, and how are they protected?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Implement scheduled access permission reviews (at least quarterly) with documented approvals.","Harden high-risk areas with dual authentication (e.g., card + PIN or card + biometric).","Integrate access control events with video surveillance and intrusion detection for correlated response.","Relocate or secure critical controllers, power supplies, and data paths inside controlled spaces.","Implement clear procedures for issuance, deactivation, and destruction of credentials."]
standards_references: ["CISA Physical Security guidance for facilities","CISA K-12 School Security Guide (access control sections)","NFPA 730 – Guide for Premises Security (access control)","FIPS 201 / NIST SP 800-116 – guidance for credential-based access"]
psa_notes: When in the field, PSAs should physically walk the perimeter and key interior zones during normal operations. Watch how people actually move: tailgating, propped doors, and informal “workarounds” are reliable indicators that the access control design does not match operational reality.

name: Radio Communications
overview: Radio communications ensure reliable, structured, and interoperable information flow during emergencies and high-tempo operations.
indicators_of_risk: ["Multiple departments using incompatible radio systems","No dedicated emergency channel or monitored channel","Radio traffic congested with non-essential chatter","No radio communication with first responders during joint incidents","Staff unaware of protocols for distress calls or emergency traffic"]
common_failures: ["Assuming radios will work under stress without prior testing","Over-reliance on one or two radios during events","Failure to program radios consistently across units","Poor antenna placement inside buildings reducing performance"]
assessment_questions: ["Do you have a dedicated emergency radio channel?","Who monitors radio traffic continuously?","How do you handle interoperability with law enforcement or fire?","Have you tested radio performance throughout the facility?","Do you practice structured radio communication during drills?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Program radios with standardized channels and naming.","Establish and enforce radio discipline and emergency traffic rules.","Conduct periodic range and reception testing.","Train staff to use radios under realistic conditions.","Integrate radios into all drills, especially multi-agency responses."]
standards_references: ["CISA SAFECOM program guidance","ICS/NIMS communications unit best practices"]
psa_notes: During walkthroughs, test radios live. Communications failures multiply rapidly during real crises.

name: JTTF Engagement
overview: Joint Terrorism Task Force (JTTF) engagement connects facilities—especially high-risk or symbolic targets—to federal, state, and local partners focused on terrorism-related threats. Appropriate engagement improves awareness of plots, tactics, and indicators relevant to the site.
indicators_of_risk: ["High-profile or high-consequence facility with no awareness of JTTF resources","Leadership unsure how to route terrorism-related tips or suspicious activity to the JTTF","No relationship with local FBI or JTTF representatives despite elevated risk profile","Facility receiving terrorism-related bulletins but not acting on them","No internal vetting process for potential terrorism-related information before external reporting"]
common_failures: ["Assuming terrorism threats are “handled by the feds” with no facility role","Over-classifying routine issues as terrorism-related, diluting credibility","Relying solely on news media for terrorism threat awareness","Not updating contact information for JTTF or FBI liaison personnel","Failing to protect sensitive law enforcement–furnished information internally"]
assessment_questions: ["Given your facility’s profile, have you engaged with the local FBI or JTTF?","Do you know how to report information that may have a terrorism nexus?","Have you received or requested terrorism-related briefings tailored to your sector?","How do you protect and limit distribution of law enforcement–sensitive information shared with you?","Can you cite any procedural or physical changes driven by JTTF-related threat information?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Identify appropriate JTTF or FBI contact routes for your facility and document them.","Train select leaders on recognizing and reporting potential terrorism-related information.","Request periodic threat briefings appropriate to your sector and risk level.","Integrate terrorism-related indicators into security training and suspicious activity reporting.","Handle law enforcement–sensitive information under need-to-know and secure storage practices."]
standards_references: ["FBI/JTTF public–private partnership guidance","DHS and CISA terrorism prevention and protective security resources"]
psa_notes: Not every facility needs deep JTTF integration, but high-value or symbolic sites should at least know who to call and what to report. Probe for realistic self-assessment of risk and actual engagement, not just awareness of acronyms.

name: Communication Protocols
overview: Communication protocols define how, when, and by whom information is transmitted during routine operations and emergencies. Clear protocols reduce confusion, duplication, and missed messages when time is critical.
indicators_of_risk: ["Staff use ad hoc methods (text chains, personal group chats) for critical communications","No documented call trees or notification sequences for emergencies","Contradictory instructions coming from multiple leaders during incidents","No distinction between routine, urgent, and emergency communications","After-action reviews repeatedly cite “poor communication” as a root cause"]
common_failures: ["Assuming people will “figure it out” without written protocols","Relying on a single individual to relay all critical information","Using jargon or codes that are not widely understood","Failing to account for off-hours, weekends, or remote personnel","Not integrating communication protocols with ICS/NIMS roles and mass notification tools"]
assessment_questions: ["How are critical incidents communicated from first observer to decision-makers?","Do you have documented call trees or contact lists with current information?","Who is authorized to send emergency messages and by what channels?","How are conflicting or duplicate messages avoided during fast-moving events?","How do you ensure off-site, night-shift, or contract personnel receive critical notifications?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Develop written communication protocols for routine, urgent, and emergency scenarios.","Align protocols with ICS/NIMS roles and mass notification capabilities.","Maintain and regularly update call trees and contact lists; test them at least annually.","Standardize message formats and use plain language wherever possible.","Include communication flow in exercises and capture lessons learned in protocol revisions."]
standards_references: ["FEMA Comprehensive Preparedness Guide (CPG) 101 – communications planning","NIMS/ICS guidance on incident communications"]
psa_notes: In interviews, ask three different people how they would communicate an unfolding threat. If the answers diverge, protocols are not institutionalized.

name: Recording / Storage (NVR/DVR)
overview: Video recording and storage systems retain evidence-quality footage for investigations, deterrence, and compliance. Reliability, retention length, and access governance determine their effectiveness.
indicators_of_risk: ["Insufficient storage leading to video overwriting before incidents are discovered","Recording gaps or missing streams due to misconfiguration or system overload","NVR/DVR units accessible to unauthorized personnel","No offsite or redundant backup for critical footage","Footage difficult to retrieve or export in usable formats"]
common_failures: ["Retention policies based on storage cost rather than operational need","Video kept for too short a period to support investigations","Maintenance issues leading to degraded or missing recordings","No chain-of-custody procedures for exported evidence"]
assessment_questions: ["What is your video retention duration, and how was it determined?","Do you have full recording coverage for critical cameras?","Who can access, download, and delete recordings?","How do you ensure NVR/DVR units are secure from tampering?","Do you test the recovery and export process periodically?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Align retention duration with investigative and regulatory needs.","Protect recording equipment in locked, access-controlled locations.","Monitor for recording failures or camera offline status.","Document procedures for evidence handling and chain of custody.","Provide redundant or cloud-based backups for critical footage."]
standards_references: ["IEC 62676 – video surveillance systems standards","CISA guidance on video surveillance system reliability and retention"]
psa_notes: PSAs should request a random playback from several days prior. If footage is missing, corrupted, or hard to extract, the system is not truly supporting security operations.

name: Interior Lighting
overview: Interior lighting supports visibility, surveillance effectiveness, safety, and situational awareness within buildings. Proper lighting reduces concealment, supports camera quality, and contributes to a secure environment.
indicators_of_risk: ["Dim corridors, storage rooms, or stairwells affecting visibility","Sensors or switches that leave areas unlit when occupied","Interior lighting causing glare or washing out camera images","Burned-out bulbs or flickering lights left unrepaired","Lighting inconsistent between adjacent areas, creating contrast shadows"]
common_failures: ["Assuming lighting is strictly a facilities function with no security role","Ignoring lighting’s impact on camera image quality","Failing to maintain consistent illumination levels across sensitive zones","Using outdated fluorescent lighting that degrades camera performance"]
assessment_questions: ["Are interior spaces adequately lit for safe movement and surveillance?","Is lighting coordinated with camera coverage and image clarity?","Are lighting repairs completed promptly?","Do sensors or timers inadvertently leave critical areas dark?","Are stairwells and emergency routes well illuminated at all hours?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Upgrade to LED lighting for consistent brightness and reliability.","Align lighting plans with camera fields of view.","Inspect lighting regularly, especially in stairwells and corridors.","Standardize fixture types and color temperatures across key areas.","Identify and eliminate shadowed or poorly lit interior zones."]
standards_references: ["IES interior lighting recommendations","NFPA 101 egress lighting requirements"]
psa_notes: Poor lighting inside is just as dangerous as poor lighting outside—check camera footage to assess real-world interior visibility.

name: Sensitive Item Storage
overview: Sensitive item storage focuses on secure control of items that could pose safety, security, or operational risk if lost, stolen, or misused—such as keys, access cards, radios, weapons, narcotics, records, or testing materials.
indicators_of_risk: ["High-value or sensitive items stored in unlocked cabinets, closets, or offices","Shared combinations or keys with no logging of who accessed storage areas","No inventory or sign-out process for sensitive equipment or materials","Sensitive items left unattended in vehicles, open workspaces, or hallways","Staff unsure who is responsible for managing and auditing sensitive storage"]
common_failures: ["Ad hoc storage using whatever space is available, with no risk assessment","Reliance on informal trust rather than actual physical controls and logs","No follow-up when items go missing or are unaccounted for","Combination codes or keys never changed despite turnover or suspected compromise"]
assessment_questions: ["What items at this site would be considered sensitive or high-risk if lost or stolen?","Where and how are these items stored when not in use?","Do you maintain inventories, sign-out logs, or electronic tracking?","Who is accountable for auditing sensitive storage areas and how often?","Have you experienced losses or unexplained discrepancies in sensitive items?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Identify categories of sensitive items and assign appropriate storage levels (locked cabinets, safes, secure rooms).","Implement sign-out and inventory control for portable sensitive items.","Restrict access to storage locations to designated, trained personnel.","Change combinations or rekey locks after turnover or suspected compromise.","Document and investigate discrepancies and losses to identify systemic issues."]
standards_references: ["CISA physical security guidance for critical assets and materials","Sector-specific regulations (e.g., healthcare, education, law enforcement) where applicable","NFPA and OSHA requirements for hazardous materials storage, as relevant to item type"]
psa_notes: Ask to see where keys, radios, master credentials, or special items are actually kept. The real sensitive-item program is whatever you observe there, not what the policy says.

name: Incident Reporting
overview: Incident reporting is the process by which staff document security, safety, and behavioral concerns. A strong reporting program turns individual observations into actionable intelligence and trend data.
indicators_of_risk: ["Staff unsure how or where to report security concerns","Reports captured informally (email, text, word-of-mouth) with no system of record","Only major incidents reported while near-misses and suspicious activity are ignored","No feedback loop to staff after they submit reports","Incident logs incomplete, inconsistent, or not retained"]
common_failures: ["Overly complex reporting forms discouraging participation","Culture that views reporting as “snitching” or administrative burden","No categorization or coding of incidents for analysis","Reports filed but never reviewed, trended, or tied to corrective actions","Reliance on a single person to manage all reporting without backup"]
assessment_questions: ["What is the formal process for reporting security or safety incidents?","How easy is it for front-line staff to submit a report during normal operations?","Who reviews reports, how often, and what decisions result?","Do you track near-misses, suspicious activity, and minor events, or only major incidents?","Can you show recent examples where incident reporting led to a change or mitigation?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Implement a simple, accessible reporting mechanism (digital, hotline, or paper) with clear instructions.","Encourage reporting of minor incidents and near-misses to identify trends early.","Categorize incidents for analysis (location, time, type, contributing factors).","Establish regular review meetings to assess reports and assign actions.","Provide feedback to staff and celebrate improvements driven by reporting."]
standards_references: ["CISA security and suspicious activity reporting best practices","Nationwide SAR Initiative (NSI) concepts adapted to facility level"]
psa_notes: Ask for the last 30–90 days of incident reports and what changed because of them. If the answer is “nothing,” the system is just record-keeping, not risk management.

name: Perimeter Lighting
overview: Perimeter lighting supports deterrence, detection, and safe operation of facility boundaries. Lighting quality directly impacts the effectiveness of cameras and guard patrols.
indicators_of_risk: ["Dark zones around fence lines, gates, or parking lots","Lighting dependent on a single circuit or not weather-protected","Burned-out fixtures unrepaired for long periods","Glare or backlighting preventing camera visibility","Fixtures mounted too low or at inappropriate angles"]
common_failures: ["Lighting installed for aesthetics rather than security function","Poor maintenance leading to sporadic or unreliable illumination","Inconsistent lighting levels creating hotspots and shadow zones","LED retrofits done without considering security implications"]
assessment_questions: ["What areas of the perimeter are lit at night?","How do you monitor and maintain perimeter lighting?","Have you performed a nighttime walk-through recently?","Is lighting coordinated with camera placement and guard patrols?","Are critical access points illuminated during power loss?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Ensure even lighting across perimeter coverage areas.","Use photocell or timer controls for consistency.","Regularly inspect lighting at night to catch real-world issues.","Integrate lighting with VSS for enhanced image quality."]
standards_references: ["IES (Illuminating Engineering Society) security lighting guidelines","CISA facility security lighting recommendations"]
psa_notes: PSAs should always evaluate lighting conditions after sunset when possible. Daytime observations miss most real deficiencies.

name: PA Systems
overview: Public Address (PA) systems broadcast real-time voice instructions for evacuation, lockdown, shelter-in-place, and routine communications. Their reliability, clarity, and reach determine how effectively occupants receive guidance.
indicators_of_risk: ["Portions of the facility where PA announcements cannot be heard","Speakers producing distortion, low volume, or inconsistent audio quality","Staff unaware of who can activate the PA system","No backup power or redundancy for PA announcements","PA not integrated with mass notification or emergency communications"]
common_failures: ["Treating PA primarily as a convenience system rather than emergency infrastructure","Not conducting intelligibility tests or volume checks","Allowing only one staff member to know activation procedures","PA unable to override background music or noise systems","Not linking PA instructions with emergency procedures"]
assessment_questions: ["Can PA announcements be clearly heard in all occupied areas?","Who is authorized and trained to issue emergency messages over PA?","Is the system regularly tested and documented?","How does the PA integrate with mass notification platforms?","Does PA have backup power for extended outages?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Conduct intelligibility tests and adjust volume and speaker placement.","Provide PA activation training to multiple staff roles.","Integrate PA with mass notification workflows and emergency scripts.","Ensure PA overrides all other audio sources during emergencies.","Add backup power and redundant communication paths."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 72 Emergency Communications Systems","CISA emergency communications and notification guidance"]
psa_notes: Walk the facility during a PA test—bathrooms, stairwells, loading docks, and mechanical spaces often have zero coverage.

name: Coordination Protocols
overview: Coordination protocols define how the facility aligns roles, responsibilities, and actions with external partners (law enforcement, fire/EMS, emergency management, neighboring facilities) before, during, and after incidents.
indicators_of_risk: ["No formal agreements or documented expectations with key external partners","Confusion at incidents over who controls the scene or makes decisions","Different agencies using incompatible procedures or terminology on-site","No joint planning meetings or exercises with external responders","Facility plans and public safety plans referencing each other inconsistently or not at all"]
common_failures: ["Relying solely on 911 as the coordination plan","Assuming public safety partners “know the facility” without tours or briefings","Developing emergency plans internally without external review","No process for joint after-action reviews following multi-agency events","Turnover in partner agencies eroding previously strong coordination links"]
assessment_questions: ["Which external agencies or partners are most critical for your emergency response?","Do you have MOUs, letters of agreement, or documented expectations with them?","How often do you conduct joint planning meetings, drills, or exercises?","How is unified command established and communicated during an incident?","Do you hold joint after-action reviews and implement shared improvements?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Identify core external partners and document coordination protocols with each.","Invite responders for site tours, pre-planning sessions, and map reviews.","Integrate facility plans with jurisdictional emergency operations plans where possible.","Conduct regular joint exercises to test coordination under stress.","Institutionalize relationships so they survive personnel turnover on both sides."]
standards_references: ["NIMS/ICS unified command principles","FEMA CPG 101 and local emergency planning guidance"]
psa_notes: Ask when the last time facility leadership and responders sat at the same table to plan or review an incident. If it has been years—or never—coordination is a vulnerability.

name: Bollards / Barriers
overview: Vehicle barriers and bollards prevent unauthorized vehicle approach, reduce ramming risk, and define controlled stand-off distances at building perimeters and entry points.
indicators_of_risk: ["Uncontrolled vehicle access directly to building entrances","Decorative bollards installed but not impact-rated or anchored","Gaps in barrier line enabling bypass by small vehicles","Barriers not aligned with threat vehicle approach paths","No maintenance or inspection of impact-rated systems"]
common_failures: ["Assuming decorative bollards provide real vehicle-stopping capability","Placing barriers too far apart or leaving open flanking routes","Failing to consider approach speed, mass, and angle of potential threats","Not coordinating barriers with pedestrian flow","Neglecting inspection of hydraulic or mechanical barrier systems"]
assessment_questions: ["Are bollards or barriers impact-rated, and to what standard?","What vehicle approach routes exist, and how are they controlled?","Are there gaps or flanking paths around installed barriers?","Are barriers inspected and maintained regularly?","Do barriers interfere with emergency vehicle access?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Use certified impact-rated bollards/barriers for high-risk areas.","Eliminate bypass routes through landscaping, fencing, or redesign.","Align barriers with realistic vehicle approach vectors.","Inspect mechanical components, anchoring, and spacing routinely.","Coordinate barrier placement with emergency services."]
standards_references: ["ASTM F2656 vehicle barrier standards","CISA vehicle ramming mitigation guidance"]
psa_notes: If bollards move when pushed by hand, they are decorative, not protective.

name: Alarm Monitoring
overview: Alarm monitoring ensures intrusion, duress, environmental, and other critical alarms are received, interpreted, and acted upon in a timely manner—either on-site, through a central station, or both.
indicators_of_risk: ["No written agreement or service-level expectations with the monitoring provider","Staff unable to describe who receives alarms after-hours and how they respond","Frequent false alarms leading to user disablement or local-only annunciation","No documented call list or outdated contact information for notifications","Alarm history not reviewed for trends, slow response, or missed events"]
common_failures: ["Assuming “the alarm company handles it” with no internal oversight","Not updating call trees and passcodes after staff turnover","Routing alarms to a non-security desk that may be unmanned or distracted","Failing to distinguish priority events (duress, perimeter breach) from low-value alerts","No periodic end-to-end testing from device activation to responder notification"]
assessment_questions: ["Who monitors your alarms during business hours and after-hours?","What are the documented response expectations for different alarm types?","How are contact lists, passcodes, and escalation paths maintained and updated?","How often do you conduct live tests with the monitoring center?","Do you receive and review detailed alarm activity reports?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Establish clear monitoring contracts or internal SOPs with defined response times.","Maintain current call lists and escalation protocols, reviewed at least annually.","Differentiate alarm priorities and ensure high-risk events receive immediate attention.","Conduct periodic, documented end-to-end alarm tests with monitoring partners.","Review alarm reports to identify nuisance patterns and opportunities for system tuning."]
standards_references: ["UL 827 – Central-Station Alarm Services","NFPA 730 – Guide for Premises Security (alarm monitoring)","CISA guidance on alarm response and monitoring coordination"]
psa_notes: Ask for the last time a live alarm test was run with the monitoring entity. If no one remembers, the “system” has never really been proven under real conditions.

name: Blast Mitigation
overview: Blast mitigation reduces the impact of explosive events on people and critical functions through standoff, structural detailing, protective glazing, and layout decisions that limit debris and progressive collapse.
indicators_of_risk: ["Uncontrolled vehicle access close to primary entrances or lobbies","Extensive unprotected glazing on lower floors facing public roads or parking","Critical functions (lobbies, reception, command spaces) located directly at exterior walls with no standoff","No blast-related considerations in recent renovations or new construction near roadways","Lack of coordination with law enforcement or antiterrorism specialists on threat scenarios"]
common_failures: ["Assuming standard building code wind or seismic design is sufficient for blast threats","Allowing drop-off lanes and parking directly against glazed facades","Concentrating occupants in glass-heavy atriums without redundancy or refuge","Adding decorative elements (planters, cladding, canopies) that create additional fragmentation hazards","Implementing isolated hardening (one wall or window) without considering overall structural response"]
assessment_questions: ["What blast or vehicle-borne threat scenarios, if any, have been considered for this facility?","How close can an unscreened vehicle approach occupied, glass-heavy, or critical areas?","Have any protective measures (glazing, structural detailing, stand-off barriers) been designed by qualified engineers?","Where are your most densely occupied spaces relative to exterior walls and public approaches?","Have blast effects been considered in continuity and mass-casualty planning?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Increase standoff distances between public vehicle areas and occupied or glass-dominated facades wherever feasible.","Use engineered exterior walls, connections, and glazing systems designed for blast loads in higher-risk facilities.","Relocate or shield critical functions away from direct line-of-sight to public roads and unsecured parking.","Use vehicle barriers, controlled road geometry, and access management to limit close-in threats.","Coordinate with qualified structural and protective design engineers when implementing blast-related projects."]
standards_references: ["UFC 3-340-02 – Structures to Resist the Effects of Accidental Explosions","FEMA 426/427/430 – reference manuals for mitigating building vulnerabilities to terrorist attacks","CISA guidance on vehicle-borne threat mitigation and site standoff"]
psa_notes: Start with standoff and vehicle control. Many sites cannot afford full engineered blast upgrades, but they can move cars and people further apart and protect the most exposed glazing and gathering areas.

name: PTZ Cameras
overview: Pan-Tilt-Zoom (PTZ) cameras allow operators to actively follow subjects, zoom for detail, or scan wide areas. They are most effective when paired with fixed cameras.
indicators_of_risk: ["PTZs set to manual mode and rarely controlled","Relying solely on PTZs where fixed coverage is needed","Preset tours too fast or too slow to capture incidents","Operators not trained to use PTZ effectively during alarms","PTZs mounted where they can be easily vandalized"]
common_failures: ["PTZs pointed at unhelpful angles for months without correction","Assuming PTZs will capture everything despite their non-continuous view","PTZ controls buried behind complex user interfaces","Failing to integrate PTZ presets with alarm events"]
assessment_questions: ["How often are PTZ tours reviewed or updated?","Do operators actively control PTZs during incidents?","What fixed cameras overlap PTZ blind spots?","Are PTZ presets linked to access control or intrusion alarms?","Are PTZs protected from tampering or repositioning?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Use fixed cameras for forensic coverage and PTZ for situational response.","Review PTZ tours quarterly to ensure relevance.","Train operators in manual PTZ control during drills.","Link PTZ presets to alarms where possible.","Install PTZs at protected heights with vandal-resistant housings."]
standards_references: ["IEC 62676 series – VSS performance and configuration","CISA VSS guidance for critical infrastructure"]
psa_notes: A PTZ that never moves or never assists decisions is essentially decorative. PSAs should ask for live demos.

name: Staff Emergency Roles
overview: Staff emergency roles define who performs critical actions during incidents—evacuation coordinators, accountability leads, communication officers, medical responders, and floor wardens.
indicators_of_risk: ["Staff unsure of their responsibilities during emergencies","Critical roles assigned informally with no documentation","Roles dependent on individuals who may not be present during all shifts","No redundancy or backup for essential positions","Training inconsistent across departments"]
common_failures: ["Assigning roles but never training or validating readiness","Relying on volunteers without formal appointment or authority","Neglecting to update assignments after turnover or reorganization","No integration of emergency roles with drills or exercises","Failing to brief new hires or contractors on emergency expectations"]
assessment_questions: ["Which staff hold emergency roles and how were they selected?","Are roles documented and accessible to all personnel?","How often are role-holders trained or evaluated?","Are backups assigned for each critical position?","Do staff understand decision authority and communications protocols?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Create a formal list of emergency roles and responsibilities.","Train role-holders annually and incorporate them into drills.","Ensure each critical role has designated alternates.","Provide quick-reference job aids and checklists.","Integrate emergency role briefings into new-hire orientation."]
standards_references: ["ICS/NIMS functional role concepts (adapted for facility level)","FEMA and OSHA emergency preparedness guidance"]
psa_notes: Ask staff directly about their emergency role. Their confidence and clarity tell you whether the plan exists only on paper.

name: Defensible Space
overview: Defensible space uses physical design, territorial cues, and social norms to promote resident, staff, or tenant ownership of areas, discouraging crime and disorder through natural guardianship.
indicators_of_risk: ["Large, ambiguous common areas where no one feels responsible for behavior or upkeep","Poorly maintained spaces with litter, graffiti, or vandalism left unaddressed","No clear distinction between public, semi-public, and private zones","Benches, alcoves, or recesses that invite loitering without oversight","Residents, staff, or tenants reporting that “no one watches” certain blocks, courtyards, or corridors"]
common_failures: ["Designing for maximum openness without defining who owns or watches each space","Ignoring early signs of disorder (trash, minor vandalism) that signal low guardianship","Relying solely on cameras or guards instead of building everyday ownership by users","Failing to coordinate security, facilities, and community stakeholders on shared spaces","Treating defensible space as a housing-only concept rather than applying it to campuses and campuses-like sites"]
assessment_questions: ["Which areas do staff or residents perceive as “no one’s responsibility”?","How are public, semi-public, and private areas differentiated by design, signage, and rules?","Are there locations where loitering, nuisance behavior, or minor crime are persistent?","Who maintains common areas and how quickly are maintenance issues addressed?","Have users been engaged in designing or improving shared spaces to increase ownership?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Clarify and mark transitions between public, semi-public, and private zones.","Align maintenance, lighting, and surveillance with areas where ownership is weak.","Engage occupants in stewardship efforts such as reporting mechanisms and improvement projects.","Reconfigure or remove features that encourage unsupervised loitering in high-risk spots.","Integrate defensible space concepts into renovation, campus planning, and housing or facility policies."]
standards_references: ["Oscar Newman’s defensible space principles and subsequent CPTED practice","CPTED guidance from law enforcement and CISA for facility and campus design"]
psa_notes: Ask front-line staff and occupants which areas they avoid or feel unsafe in. Their answers usually map directly onto weak defensible space and low ownership.

name: Fusion Center Interface
overview: Fusion centers provide threat intelligence and situational awareness across jurisdictions. Facilities connected to their fusion center receive timely, relevant threat information that improves preparedness.
indicators_of_risk: ["Facility not enrolled in any fusion center distribution lists","No awareness of threat bulletins or suspicious activity reporting","Leadership unsure what a fusion center is or how to use it","No designated intelligence point-of-contact within the facility"]
common_failures: ["Facilities subscribe to fusion center bulletins but never read or act on them","Threat trend information not incorporated into risk assessments or training","No feedback loop: facility never sends SAR tips upstream"]
assessment_questions: ["Are you currently connected to a state or regional fusion center?","How do you receive intelligence updates or threat bulletins?","Who is responsible for reviewing and acting on received intelligence?","Have you ever sent information back to the fusion center?","How is intelligence incorporated into your emergency planning?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Enroll facility leadership in fusion center distribution channels.","Designate an intelligence officer or point-of-contact within the facility.","Integrate threat bulletins into training, drills, and risk assessments.","Develop a simple SAR reporting procedure for staff."]
standards_references: ["Fusion Center Guidelines (DHS/DOJ)","Nationwide SAR Initiative","CISA Security Planning resources"]
psa_notes: PSAs should verify that facilities not only receive but act on intelligence. A folder full of unread PDFs is not an intelligence program.

name: Sightlines
overview: Sightlines describe how well staff, security personnel, and cameras can see approaches, entries, corridors, and gathering spaces. Good sightlines support deterrence, early detection, and rapid assessment of suspicious behavior.
indicators_of_risk: ["Entrances, courtyards, or play areas obscured by landscaping, walls, or structures","Long interior corridors with no passive oversight from staffed locations","Hidden alcoves, recessed doorways, or blind corners near access points","Parking areas, bus loops, or drop-off zones not visible from occupied spaces","Security cameras placed but blocked by signs, banners, trees, or architectural features"]
common_failures: ["Landscaping and site furniture installed with aesthetics in mind but no security review","Renovations and additions creating new blind spots that were never reassessed","Security relying solely on cameras instead of combining natural and mechanical surveillance","Interior window coverings left closed, eliminating valuable passive observation"]
assessment_questions: ["From where can staff or security see your primary approaches and entries?","Are there exterior areas where people can loiter without being seen?","Which interior corridors or stairwells are hardest to supervise?","Have you adjusted landscaping, signage, or fixtures to improve sightlines?","Do you periodically reassess sightlines after changes to the site or building?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Trim or remove vegetation and objects that block views of entrances and gathering areas.","Add windows, vision panels, or internal openings to allow passive observation where appropriate.","Use mirrors or cameras to cover unavoidable blind corners and hidden areas.","Align guard posts, reception desks, and staff work areas with key lines of sight.","Include sightline analysis in design and renovation reviews using CPTED principles."]
