standards_references: ["CPTED (Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design) guidance on natural surveillance","CISA soft target and crowded places security resources","CISA K-12 School Security Guide – campus design considerations"]
psa_notes: PSAs should literally walk the site and building with “offender eyes”: note where a person could approach, wait, or work unseen. Those locations should drive sightline improvements or added surveillance.

name: Motion Detectors
overview: Motion detectors provide interior intrusion detection coverage by sensing movement within secured spaces, typically using PIR, microwave, or dual-technology sensors.
indicators_of_risk: ["Coverage gaps in hallways, storage rooms, or sensitive areas","High false-alarm rates due to poor placement or calibration","Detectors installed near HVAC vents, windows, or heat sources","Lack of integration with intrusion alarm panels","Sensors obstructed by shelving, furniture, or temporary storage"]
common_failures: ["Improper mounting height or angle causing missed detection","Using PIR-only detectors in high-temperature fluctuation areas","Failure to update coverage after layout changes","No annual functionality testing or calibration"]
assessment_questions: ["Are motion detectors placed according to manufacturer guidelines?","Are dual-tech sensors installed in environmentally unstable areas?","Is sensor coverage mapped and documented?","What is the false-alarm rate and how is it managed?","Are detectors integrated with the central alarm system?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Use dual-technology detectors in areas prone to false alarms.","Map coverage and eliminate blind spots.","Perform annual or semi-annual sensor testing.","Avoid placing detectors near vents, heaters, or windows.","Ensure sensors are tamper-protected and alarm-reporting capable."]
standards_references: ["UL 639 Intrusion-Detection Units","ASIS Physical Security Guidelines"]
psa_notes: Walk interior areas and look for blind spots, environmental issues, and detector obstructions. Ask to see recent false-alarm logs.

name: Interoperable Communications
overview: Interoperable communications allow security, facilities, leadership, and external responders to communicate across compatible systems during emergencies. This includes radio cross-banding, shared channels, and unified command communication protocols.
indicators_of_risk: ["Security, facilities, and leadership on separate radio systems without cross-communication capability","No shared channel or protocol with law enforcement or fire services","Radios incompatible during mutual aid or multi-agency response","Staff unaware of when and how to switch to interoperable channels","Dead zones or poor coverage in critical facility areas"]
common_failures: ["Assuming interoperability exists when it has never been tested","Relying on cell phones instead of hardened communication paths","Failing to coordinate radio templates with public safety agencies","No training on use of shared or emergency channels","Not documenting communication roles in ICS/NIMS structure"]
assessment_questions: ["What communication pathways exist between internal teams?","How do you communicate with responding agencies during incidents?","Have interoperable channels been tested recently?","Do all critical personnel carry compatible devices?","How are communication failures escalated or mitigated?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Establish shared channels with public safety partners.","Test interoperable communication protocols quarterly.","Provide cross-band or gateway solutions where practical.","Train staff on channel switching and communications discipline.","Map and fix radio dead zones inside the facility."]
standards_references: ["DHS SAFECOM interoperability guidance","ICS/NIMS communications unit leadership principles"]
psa_notes: Have them perform a live radio test with an external responder. Most organizations fail—they’ve never tested their assumptions.

name: Door Contacts
overview: Door contacts detect the opening of protected doors and are foundational to intrusion detection, especially for after-hours protection of perimeter and high-risk interior doors.
indicators_of_risk: ["Critical exterior or interior doors with no sign of alarm contact or cabling","Contacts visibly damaged, bypassed, or painted over","Staff unsure what happens if a protected door is forced after hours","Alarm annunciators located in unmanned or rarely occupied areas","Frequent nuisance alarms leading staff to ignore or disable the system"]
common_failures: ["Not all perimeter doors are monitored, particularly secondary or infrequently used exits","Doors propped open during business hours without any alarm or local alert","Alarm signals not communicated to a central point or remote monitoring center","No documented response procedures for alarm events","No periodic testing of contacts, panels, or signaling paths"]
assessment_questions: ["Which doors are monitored by the intrusion system, and how was that determined?","What is the procedure when an after-hours door alarm activates?","How often are alarm points and signaling paths tested?","Do you track nuisance alarms, and how are they mitigated?","Is intrusion monitoring provided on-site, off-site, or both?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Ensure all primary perimeter doors and critical interior doors are monitored with functional contacts.","Establish periodic testing and documentation of intrusion points and communications.","Define and train on clear response procedures for after-hours alarms.","Reduce nuisance alarms through maintenance, better door hardware, and policy enforcement.","Integrate intrusion events with video and access control where feasible."]
standards_references: ["UL 681 – Installation and classification of burglar and holdup alarm systems","CISA security guidance for facilities with alarmed perimeters","NFPA 730 – premises security (intrusion sections)"]
psa_notes: Door contacts are often the most basic element of the system but also the most neglected. PSAs should check actual door behavior: if doors are routinely propped open or alarms frequently ignored, the control is effectively non-functional regardless of what the panel shows.

name: Door Readers
overview: Door readers verify access credentials such as proximity cards, smart cards, mobile credentials, or fobs to permit entry. They form the core of electronic access control systems and must be installed and configured to minimize tampering and unauthorized access.
indicators_of_risk: ["Readers installed without protective housings","Cabling exposed or accessible from the exterior side of a door","Reader easily vandalized or bypassed","No logging of access attempts or denied entries","Readers not integrated with access control management software"]
common_failures: ["Installing readers too close to door hardware, enabling skimming or tamper access","Improper mounting height or angle reducing read reliability","Using unencrypted card technologies vulnerable to cloning","Failing to maintain firmware or software for reader updates"]
assessment_questions: ["What credential technology is used (Prox, iClass, DESFire, mobile)?","Are readers tamper-resistant and properly installed?","Is access logged and reviewed regularly?","How are denied access attempts monitored?","Are readers updated for security patches or encryption upgrades?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Transition from legacy Prox to encrypted smartcard/mobile credentials.","Protect cabling and install tamper-resistant housings.","Review access logs and denied attempts for anomalies.","Ensure proper mounting height and sightline clearance.","Maintain firmware and apply security patches."]
standards_references: ["OSDP Secure Channel Protocol Guidelines","ASIS Access Control System Standards"]
psa_notes: Look for outdated Prox card readers — they are trivial to clone with $50 devices. Upgrading card tech is a high-impact, low-disruption improvement.

name: Radios / Two-Way
overview: Two-way radios provide real-time communication among staff, security, and responders. Reliable radios significantly improve coordination during emergencies and routine operations.
indicators_of_risk: ["Dead zones where radios cannot transmit or receive","Staff unsure of radio procedures or proper channels","No radios assigned to critical roles (front desk, administrators, supervisors)","Batteries frequently dead due to lack of charging discipline","Mixing incompatible radio models, channels, or encryption standards"]
common_failures: ["Relying solely on cell phones despite network overload during emergencies","No radio discipline, leading to chatter or unclear transmissions","Radios kept in drawers instead of carried during operations","Failure to test radios daily or prior to high-risk events"]
assessment_questions: ["Which staff have radios during normal and emergency operations?","Where are your known dead zones and how are they addressed?","Do you have clear radio procedures and call signs?","How are radios charged, tracked, and maintained?","Do you practice radio communication during drills?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Ensure critical roles carry radios at all times.","Establish radio procedures, brevity codes, and call signs.","Map dead zones and deploy repeaters or reposition antennas.","Create a charging and maintenance schedule with accountability.","Integrate radios into drills, especially lockdown and evacuation operations."]
standards_references: ["CISA Emergency Communications Best Practices","NFPA 1221 – emergency communications infrastructure"]
psa_notes: Ask staff to demonstrate a live radio call. If radios are dead, missing, or unused, real emergencies will suffer badly.

name: Visitor Management Systems
overview: Visitor Management Systems (VMS) track and control the entry of non-employee individuals through pre-registration, ID verification, badge issuance, and logging of visit details. They help maintain accountability and reduce unauthorized access.
indicators_of_risk: ["Visitors allowed to enter without being logged or recorded","Paper logs used instead of digital systems, making audit difficult","Temporary badges not visually distinct from employee badges","Reception unable to verify visitor identity or host approval","Expired visitor badges not collected upon exit"]
common_failures: ["No requirement for visitors to show identification","Visitor logs stored insecurely or incompletely filled out","Not notifying hosts when their visitor arrives","Allowing visitors to self-escort through secure areas","Failure to reconcile visitor departures at the end of day"]
assessment_questions: ["Is a visitor management system digital or paper-based?","Are visitors required to present ID before entry?","Are badges clearly marked and differentiated from staff?","Does the system track check-in and check-out times?","Are visitor records retained for a documented period?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Implement a digital VMS with ID scanning and badge printing.","Require host pre-approval for visitor access.","Enforce visual differentiation between visitor and employee badges.","Train reception on identity verification protocols.","Secure and retain visitor records according to policy."]
standards_references: ["ASIS Visitor Management Guidelines","ISC Physical Access Criteria – Visitor Processing"]
psa_notes: Observe live reception operations. Most facilities claim to use VMS, but real enforcement is often inconsistent or bypassed.

name: Monitoring / Workstations
overview: Monitoring workstations support real-time surveillance, alarm monitoring, and security decision-making. Operator workload, layout, and system configuration directly affect response effectiveness.
indicators_of_risk: ["Operators responsible for excessive camera or alarm loads","Improper ergonomics or workstation layout","Critical alarms not acknowledged or logged","Inadequate lighting or screen visibility","Unsecured monitoring stations accessible to non-security personnel"]
common_failures: ["Using reception staff as primary monitoring personnel","No redundancy or backup monitoring location","Poorly configured alarm rules leading to overload","Lack of training on systems and incident procedures"]
assessment_questions: ["Is workstation access restricted to authorized personnel?","Is the operator-to-camera ratio within recognized limits?","Are alarms logged, escalated, and reviewed?","Is the workstation ergonomically designed to reduce fatigue?","Is there a backup console or secondary monitoring point?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Limit operator workstation access to authorized staff.","Optimize alarm rules to reduce unnecessary triggers.","Conduct operator training and certification.","Implement ergonomic improvements to reduce fatigue.","Establish secondary or redundant monitoring capability."]
standards_references: ["ISO 11064 Control Room Design Standards","ASIS Security Operations Center Guidelines"]
psa_notes: Evaluate visibility, workload, room layout, and alarm-handling procedures. Ask operators to demonstrate live alarm acknowledgment.

name: Hard-Interior Barriers
overview: Hard interior barriers provide physical separation and delay between public or low-security zones and critical assets. They include walls, reinforced partitions, security glazing, and compartmentalization structures intended to slow adversary movement.
indicators_of_risk: ["Critical rooms protected only by drywall or hollow-core partitions","No physical delay between public spaces and sensitive operations","Unsecured ceiling plenums enabling bypass over walls","Weak construction around high-value rooms (IT, pharmaceuticals, cash areas)","Renovations that accidentally reduce security segregation"]
common_failures: ["Relying solely on access control without physical protection","Ignoring wall-to-ceiling height, leaving open bypass routes","Using office-grade partitions for high-risk spaces","Not coordinating remodels with security implications","No assessment of wall or barrier construction in critical zones"]
assessment_questions: ["What physical separation exists between public areas and sensitive assets?","Do walls extend to the true ceiling or deck above?","Are high-value rooms reinforced or hardened?","Were barrier requirements considered during renovations?","How long would the barrier realistically delay forced entry?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Install reinforced walls or upgraded construction around critical rooms.","Ensure walls extend to the structural ceiling to prevent bypass.","Use security glazing or barrier film for visibility and delay.","Review interior segmentation during all facility modifications.","Incorporate CPTED and forced-entry resistance principles."]
standards_references: ["ASTM forced-entry resistance standards","CISA facility hardening guidelines"]
psa_notes: Hard barriers are the difference between “access control” and “actual protection.” Verify wall construction physically—don’t assume.

name: Perimeter IDS
overview: Perimeter Intrusion Detection Systems (PIDS) detect attempts to breach fences, walls, or defined boundaries using technologies such as fence vibration sensors, buried cable, microwave, or infrared beams. They provide early warning before an adversary reaches the building.
indicators_of_risk: ["Critical perimeters (fuel farms, yards, sensitive storage) unmonitored outside of guard hours","PIDS equipment installed but routinely bypassed or disabled due to nuisance alarms","No clear zoning, making it difficult to locate the source of an alarm along the perimeter","Vegetation, debris, or loose fence fabric degrading sensor performance","No integration between PIDS alarms, lighting, and camera presets"]
common_failures: ["Treating PIDS as a technology project without defining response protocols","Poor installation on flimsy or poorly tensioned fences","Ignoring manufacturer guidelines for zone length, tension, and environmental conditions","Allowing long-term nuisance alarms to train staff to ignore signals","Not revisiting PIDS design after site changes, new buildings, or altered traffic flows"]
assessment_questions: ["Which perimeter segments are covered by PIDS and which are not? Why?","How are PIDS alarms received, displayed, and acknowledged?","What is the documented response requirement for a perimeter alarm (time, actions)?","How frequently are PIDS zones tested and maintenance performed?","Are PIDS events correlated with cameras, lighting, and guard patrol routes?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Align PIDS deployment with a clearly defined response concept and guard/SOC procedures.","Ensure fences and physical boundaries meet basic structural requirements before adding sensors.","Tune zones to reduce nuisance alarms and maintain operator trust.","Integrate PIDS with VSS, lighting, and alarm monitoring to support rapid verification.","Conduct regular walk-tests and maintenance with both security and facilities involved."]
standards_references: ["IEC / ISO guidance for perimeter intrusion detection systems","CENELEC EN 50131 family (intrusion and hold-up systems) where applicable","CISA perimeter security and PIDS best-practice resources"]
psa_notes: Ask to see recent PIDS alarm history. A log full of unresolved nuisance alarms or long periods of silence on a busy perimeter both point to systemic issues.

name: Security Policies
overview: Security policies define the organization’s expectations and requirements for protecting people, assets, and information. They set the framework for procedures, training, and enforcement.
indicators_of_risk: ["Security policies outdated, referencing staff, systems, or structures that no longer exist","Policies written at corporate level with no local adaptation for site conditions","Staff unaware that security policies exist or where to find them","Policies conflict with operational realities, encouraging routine non-compliance","No formal process for approving, reviewing, and retiring policies"]
common_failures: ["Policies created reactively after incidents but not integrated into daily practices","Security policies scattered across multiple documents with inconsistent terminology","Policy scope unclear—staff unsure which rules apply to them","No enforcement or consequence for repeated policy violations"]
assessment_questions: ["Which documents define your physical security policies for this site?","When were they last reviewed and by whom?","How are policy updates communicated to staff and contractors?","Do you track and respond to repeated policy violations?","How do site-specific realities feed back into policy changes?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Establish a clear hierarchy of security policies and ensure they are accessible.","Implement a regular review cycle (e.g., annually or after major incidents).","Align policies with actual site operations and staffing levels.","Communicate changes through training, briefings, and updated job aids.","Use incident and violation data to guide policy refinement."]
standards_references: ["CISA physical security best practices and governance guidance","ASIS guidelines on security program management and policy frameworks","ISO 27001/22301-style management practices adapted to physical security policies"]
psa_notes: PSAs should check whether policies drive practice or merely exist for auditors. Real effectiveness shows up in culture and daily decisions, not just documents.

name: Exterior Cameras
overview: Exterior cameras provide video coverage for building perimeters, parking areas, entry routes, loading docks, and other outdoor zones. Their effectiveness depends on placement, environmental protection, and nighttime performance.
indicators_of_risk: ["Cameras mounted too high or far to capture identifying details","Nighttime scenes washed out due to poor lighting or IR reflection","Environmental damage such as water ingress or sun glare","Blind spots around corners, shrubs, or vehicles","Exterior cameras offline with no alerts"]
common_failures: ["Installing cameras without considering sun paths or weather exposure","Not using outdoor-rated housings or surge protection","Relying solely on IR illumination instead of scene lighting","Failing to inspect lenses for dirt, insects, or moisture","Placing cameras where they can be easily vandalized"]
assessment_questions: ["Do exterior cameras provide facial or vehicle identification?","Is coverage continuous around the building perimeter?","How do cameras perform at night and during weather conditions?","Are housings rated and maintained for outdoor use?","Is exterior lighting supporting camera image quality?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Reposition cameras for optimal angle and identification distance.","Add or improve exterior lighting for nighttime clarity.","Use weatherproof housings and anti-vandal mounts.","Regularly clean lenses and check for environmental damage.","Ensure perimeter coverage has no blind zones."]
standards_references: ["DHS Exterior Video Surveillance Guidelines","SIA Outdoor Camera Installation Standards"]
psa_notes: Nighttime image quality is the real test. Review actual dark-hour footage rather than relying on daytime performance.

name: School Resource Officer (SRO)
overview: School Resource Officers (SROs) are sworn law enforcement officers assigned to schools. Their role, when properly scoped, combines safety, relationship-building, and coordinated response.
indicators_of_risk: ["No clear memorandum of understanding (MOU) defining SRO roles and limits","SRO used primarily for general discipline or administrative tasks","SRO excluded from emergency planning, drills, and after-action reviews","Students and staff unclear about how to appropriately engage the SRO","No continuity plan when the assigned SRO is absent or reassigned"]
common_failures: ["Ambiguous or conflicting expectations between school leadership and law enforcement","Over-reliance on SRO presence instead of improving baseline preventive measures","SRO outreach and relationship-building neglected due to workload","No performance or impact review of the SRO program beyond anecdote"]
assessment_questions: ["Do you have a current MOU that outlines the SRO’s mission, authority, and limitations?","How is the SRO integrated into drills, briefings, and threat assessment processes?","How are student and staff interactions with the SRO guided and communicated?","What training does the SRO receive specific to school environments and youth interaction?","How do you evaluate the effectiveness of the SRO program?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Develop and maintain a clear MOU between the school/district and law enforcement agency.","Clarify that the SRO complements, not replaces, broader safety and climate efforts.","Integrate the SRO into planning, exercises, and threat assessment teams.","Provide training on adolescent development, de-escalation, and trauma-informed practices.","Review SRO program outcomes periodically with both school and law enforcement leadership."]
standards_references: ["COPS Office and NASRO best practices for SRO programs","CISA K-12 School Security Guide – law enforcement partnership sections"]
psa_notes: An SRO program is only as good as its structure and relationships. PSAs should focus on governance, integration, and culture—not just presence.

name: Risk Assessment / Risk Management
overview: Risk assessment and risk management provide a structured way to identify threats, vulnerabilities, and consequences, then prioritize and track mitigation efforts over time.
indicators_of_risk: ["No documented security or risk assessment for the facility","Assessments conducted once for a project and never updated","Mitigation recommendations not tracked to completion or revisited","Decisions driven solely by recent incidents rather than systematic risk analysis","No linkage between risk findings and budget, training, or design decisions"]
common_failures: ["Treating risk assessments as one-time compliance tasks, not ongoing processes","Focusing only on physical measures while ignoring people and process vulnerabilities","Risk registers or action plans existing but not used in decision-making","Failure to include multi-disciplinary perspectives in risk discussions"]
assessment_questions: ["When was your last formal risk or security assessment and what did it cover?","How are identified vulnerabilities and recommendations tracked?","Who participates in risk assessment and prioritization decisions?","How do risk assessment findings influence budgets, projects, and policies?","Do you revisit risk after major changes in layout, occupancy, or threat environment?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Establish a recurring risk assessment cycle appropriate to facility risk level.","Document vulnerabilities, options for consideration, and chosen mitigations in a trackable format.","Engage cross-functional stakeholders (operations, safety, IT, legal, etc.) in risk discussions.","Use risk findings to prioritize investments in people, processes, and technology.","Update risk assessments after significant incidents, renovations, or mission changes."]
standards_references: ["CISA Risk Management Process for Federal Facilities (adapted as appropriate)","ISO 31000 – risk management principles (as a framework)","Sector-specific risk assessment guides (e.g., for schools, healthcare, energy)"]
psa_notes: If there is no clear line of sight from risk findings to concrete changes, the assessment is just a report. PSAs should look for that linkage explicitly.

name: Policy Compliance Tracking
overview: Policy compliance tracking ensures that security and safety policies are followed consistently across all departments and shifts. It helps organizations identify gaps and improve accountability.
indicators_of_risk: ["Policies written but rarely enforced or monitored","No audit trail showing who completed required tasks","Supervisors unaware of compliance responsibilities","Compliance handled informally via verbal reminders","Repeated violations with no corrective action"]
common_failures: ["Policies developed in isolation without operational input","Tracking done manually with inconsistent data","Compliance logs never reviewed for trends","Policies contradicting workload or staffing realities"]
assessment_questions: ["How do you track compliance with key security policies?","Do you audit compliance regularly? By whom?","How do you address repeated violations?","Are compliance responsibilities assigned by role?","Do compliance findings drive procedural or policy changes?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Develop simple, role-based checklists and compliance logs.","Conduct periodic audits and communicate findings.","Align compliance expectations with operations and staffing.","Automate compliance tracking where possible.","Use compliance trends to drive training and policy updates."]
standards_references: ["CISA physical security program management guidance","ASIS security governance principles"]
psa_notes: Policies mean nothing without enforcement. PSAs should ask to see real compliance logs, not templates.

name: Territorial Reinforcement
overview: Territorial reinforcement uses physical features, signage, pavement changes, and landscaping to express ownership and guide behavior. Clear territorial cues discourage unauthorized use of space.
indicators_of_risk: ["Confusing or ambiguous boundaries between public and private areas","Lack of signage marking restricted or staff-only zones","Shared spaces where no one perceives responsibility (“no man’s land” areas)","Entrances or pathways that look public but lead to sensitive areas","Facilities blending indistinguishably into surrounding public space"]
common_failures: ["Facilities avoid signage due to aesthetics or fear of “unwelcoming” appearance","Public areas expand informally into semi-private or private zones","Parking areas used by non-affiliated individuals without oversight"]
assessment_questions: ["How do visitors know where they are allowed to go?","What design elements communicate ownership or controlled space?","Are restricted areas clearly marked?","Have you had issues with loitering or unauthorized use of spaces?","Do staff feel responsible for monitoring adjacent exterior areas?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Use signage, barriers, and pavement changes to clearly communicate transitions.","Define public, semi-public, and private zones.","Enhance visibility of entrances and reduce ambiguous access points.","Integrate CPTED principles into renovations and expansions."]
standards_references: ["CPTED principles for territorial reinforcement","CISA facility boundary definition guidance"]
psa_notes: Territorial cues influence behavior subconsciously. PSAs should assess whether spaces feel “owned” or “ambiguous” — ambiguity is a risk amplifier.

name: Emergency Drills
overview: Emergency drills test the organization’s readiness to perform evacuations, lockdowns, shelter-in-place, and other protective actions. Effective drills validate plans, identify weaknesses, and build staff confidence.
indicators_of_risk: ["Drills conducted only for compliance with minimal realism","Staff unaware of their roles during drills","No documentation or after-action reviews following exercises","Drills always pre-announced, predictable, or performed at low-occupancy times","No integration of external responders or multi-department coordination"]
common_failures: ["Relying exclusively on fire drills and ignoring other emergency types","Failing to adapt drills to different times, seasons, or operational conditions","Not addressing weaknesses identified in previous exercises","Running drills without evaluating leadership decision-making","Excluding contractors, visitors, and part-time staff from drill participation"]
assessment_questions: ["What types of drills are conducted and how often?","How are lessons learned captured and tracked to closure?","Are drills designed to test realistic scenarios and decision-making?","Do drills include evaluation of communications and coordination?","How is staff performance measured or scored during drills?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Conduct multi-hazard drills: evacuation, lockdown, shelter-in-place, and medical scenarios.","Vary timing and integrate realistic injects to test adaptability.","Perform structured after-action reviews with corrective action tracking.","Include external partners such as local law enforcement or fire services.","Ensure all personnel categories participate, including contractors and part-time staff."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 1600 – Emergency Management and Business Continuity","FEMA exercise design principles (HSEEP framework)","CISA active shooter preparedness and drills guidance"]
psa_notes: Ask for after-action reports. If there are none—or if the same findings repeat—drills aren’t improving readiness.

name: Security Training Programs
overview: Security training programs provide structured learning for staff at all levels on threats, procedures, reporting, and use of security systems. They are the primary tool for turning written plans into real-world capability.
indicators_of_risk: ["No formal training calendar for security or emergency topics","Training delivered once at hire and never refreshed","Front-line staff unaware of how to report suspicious behavior or incidents","Training focused purely on compliance slides rather than practical scenarios","High-turnover roles (reception, contract guards, substitutes) under-trained"]
common_failures: ["Assuming an annual online module is sufficient for complex emergencies","Leadership receiving more robust training than front-line staff who actually respond first","No evaluation of training effectiveness beyond attendance records","Failure to incorporate lessons learned from incidents or drills into future training content"]
assessment_questions: ["What is your annual training plan for security and emergency preparedness?","Which groups receive which content (leadership, staff, contractors, volunteers)?","How do you evaluate whether training actually changed behavior or knowledge?","How are new hires and temporary staff integrated into training?","Can you point to a training change that resulted from an incident or exercise?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Build a multi-year training plan that covers awareness, procedures, and system use.","Use scenario-based training and tabletop exercises to make content realistic.","Tailor training modules for different roles and responsibilities.","Track not just attendance, but also performance and behavioral indicators.","Update training content regularly based on observed gaps and evolving threats."]
standards_references: ["CISA Security 101 and security awareness resources","FEMA and DHS training materials for emergency preparedness","ASIS and sector-specific training best practices"]
psa_notes: A strong training program is reflected in how confidently staff describe what they would do. PSAs should rely more on staff interviews and observed competence than on training binders.

name: Fixed Cameras
overview: Fixed cameras provide continuous surveillance over specific zones with unchanging fields of view. They are essential for monitoring entrances, chokepoints, assets, and sensitive areas.
indicators_of_risk: ["Cameras pointed incorrectly or drifted from intended angles","Obstructed views due to signage, vegetation, or poor placement","Outdated low-resolution cameras unable to provide identification","Cameras offline with no alerting or monitoring","Insufficient nighttime performance due to lighting issues"]
common_failures: ["Installing cameras too high or too far from the target area","Incorrect lens choice leading to distorted or unusable footage","Failing to document camera placement or intended purpose","Not integrating fixed cameras with video analytics or alarms"]
assessment_questions: ["Are fixed cameras capturing their intended coverage area?","Is the image quality sufficient for identification tasks?","Are cameras inspected, cleaned, and tested regularly?","Do cameras provide reliable nighttime performance?","Are fixed cameras supported by adequate lighting?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Re-aim and document fixed camera coverage areas.","Upgrade outdated low-resolution units to modern HD/4K hardware.","Ensure lighting supports nighttime image clarity.","Establish periodic camera cleaning and function checks.","Integrate cameras with access and intrusion alerts."]
standards_references: ["SIA Video Surveillance Performance Standards","DHS CCTV Best Practices for Critical Facilities"]
psa_notes: Physically inspect camera angles — many are misaimed due to impacts, wind, or poor installation. Live verification is essential.

name: Backup Communications
overview: Backup communications ensure the facility can send and receive critical messages during power outages, system failures, network disruptions, or emergencies where primary communications are degraded.
indicators_of_risk: ["No secondary communication method if radios or phones fail","Backup systems untested or unknown to staff","Dependence on a single cellular carrier with poor redundancy","Satellite or radio backups present but not maintained or powered","Critical staff unaware of how to activate backup systems"]
common_failures: ["Purchasing backup systems and letting them sit unused until failure","Failing to rotate batteries or test satellite/analog equipment","Assuming text messaging will work during major incidents","No plan for communicating with offsite leadership during outages","Backup systems dependent on the same power or network as primary systems"]
assessment_questions: ["What backup communication systems exist (radio, satellite, analog)?","How often are these systems tested and who performs the tests?","Do backups operate independently of primary network and power?","Are backup instructions documented and accessible?","How do staff escalate communication failures?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Establish redundant communications: satellite, analog radios, backup PA systems.","Test backups monthly and document readiness.","Provide battery backups or independent power sources.","Train key staff on activation and use of backup tools.","Ensure backup communications reach leadership and external partners."]
standards_references: ["FEMA continuity communications guidelines","CISA considerations for communications resilience"]
psa_notes: Backup communications are binary—they either work or they don’t. Live testing is the only truth.

name: Secure Rooms
overview: Secure rooms protect critical functions, sensitive information, or valuable assets through enhanced physical construction, access control, and monitoring.
indicators_of_risk: ["Rooms designated as “secure” but with standard interior doors and hardware","Access controlled electronically but with unsecured adjacent ceilings, walls, or windows","Sensitive equipment or information stored in ordinary offices due to space constraints","No logging or review of access to secure rooms","Secure rooms used informally for general storage or meetings"]
common_failures: ["Security upgrades focused only on the door, ignoring wall, ceiling, and glazing weaknesses","Secure room purposes changing over time without reassessment","Temporary access granted and never revoked","No backup plan if secure room access control systems fail"]
assessment_questions: ["Which rooms are considered secure, and what is their purpose?","What structural or hardware measures differentiate them from normal rooms?","How is access granted, logged, and reviewed?","Have you assessed alternative attack paths (e.g., ceiling, adjacent rooms, glazing)?","What is your contingency plan if the secure room cannot be accessed or must be bypassed in an emergency?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Align secure room construction with the sensitivity of the functions or assets inside.","Use strengthened walls, doors, frames, and locking hardware where justified.","Restrict and log access, with periodic reviews of access lists and audit trails.","Assess adjacent spaces and above-ceiling pathways for bypass risk.","Ensure emergency responders understand how to access or bypass secure rooms when necessary."]
standards_references: ["CISA guidance on secure areas and critical room protection","Sector- or mission-specific secure room standards where applicable (e.g., SCIF/records rooms for certain environments)"]
psa_notes: The term “secure” is often aspirational. PSAs should focus on real construction, hardware, and access governance, not labels on floor plans.

name: Glass Break Sensors
overview: Glass break sensors detect forced entry through windows or glass doors. They are important for facilities with extensive glazing or vulnerable ground-level windows.
indicators_of_risk: ["Large glass areas with no visible or documented glass break detection","Sensors present but staff unsure if they are functional","False alarms during storms or loud noises leading to disabled sensors","Ground-floor windows unprotected in isolated areas"]
common_failures: ["Only perimeter doors monitored, ignoring window vulnerabilities","Improper sensor placement leading to poor detection","No integration with monitoring centers for rapid response"]
assessment_questions: ["Which glass areas are protected and why?","When were glass break detectors last tested?","Have false alarms been an issue? How were they addressed?","Does the facility rely solely on window locks without detection?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Protect ground-level and easily accessible windows with glass break sensors.","Test sensors semi-annually, especially after major weather.","Integrate detection with VSS for immediate verification.","Use laminated or impact-resistant glazing where risk is high."]
standards_references: ["UL 639 – Intrusion Detection Units","CISA guidance on perimeter intrusion protection"]
psa_notes: Facilities with large glass storefronts or student-facing areas are often surprised by how quickly a window breach can occur. Sensors are a low-cost, high-impact layer.

name: Restricted Keys
overview: Restricted keys prevent unauthorized duplication and limit physical access to critical spaces. They rely on controlled issuance, secure storage, and strict tracking processes.
indicators_of_risk: ["Master keys or sub-master keys stored in unlocked desks or common areas","Key blanks easily duplicated at local hardware stores due to lack of patent protection","No record of who has which keys or when they were last returned","Lost keys not triggering rekeying or security review","Contractors or former staff retaining keys after project completion or departure"]
common_failures: ["Assuming electronic access control eliminates the need for key control","Issuing keys broadly “just in case” with no justification or expiration","Failure to secure mechanical overrides for electronically controlled doors","Relying on paper logs that are incomplete or never audited"]
assessment_questions: ["Do you use patented or restricted keyways to prevent unauthorized duplication?","How do you track key issuance, return, and loss?","Where are master keys stored and who has access to them?","Do you have a defined process for rekeying when keys are lost?","How are contractors or temporary staff managed from a key control perspective?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Move to restricted or patented keyway systems to prevent unauthorized copying.","Use electronic or well-maintained manual logging to track issuance and return.","Secure master and sub-master keys in locked, access-controlled storage.","Implement rekeying protocols for lost or compromised keys.","Review key access lists regularly and remove unnecessary access."]
standards_references: ["CISA physical access control best practices","NFPA 730 – premises security key control recommendations"]
psa_notes: Ask to see the key cabinet and issuance records. If they are disorganized, outdated, or nonexistent, key security is weak regardless of stated policies.

name: Exterior Lighting
overview: Exterior lighting supports deterrence, detection, and safe circulation around building façades, entries, and parking areas. It is a core enabler for both natural surveillance and video effectiveness after dark.
indicators_of_risk: ["Dark or shadowed areas at main entries, walkways, or parking lots","Lighting levels highly uneven, with bright hotspots and deep shadows","Fixtures placed so they create glare for guards, drivers, or cameras","Burned-out or damaged fixtures not replaced for extended periods","Lighting controlled manually or by inconsistent schedules instead of photocells or timers"]
common_failures: ["Treating exterior lighting purely as an aesthetic design feature","Retrofitting to LEDs without verifying aiming, color temperature, and uniformity for security use","Failing to coordinate lighting design with camera fields of view and patrol routes","Not inspecting lighting conditions at night, relying only on daytime observations","Allowing vegetation, banners, or signage to block or degrade light coverage"]
assessment_questions: ["Which exterior areas are intentionally illuminated for security and safety, and how was that determined?","Do you conduct nighttime inspections to assess real-world lighting conditions?","How quickly are lighting outages reported and repaired?","Is lighting coordinated with video surveillance coverage and guard patrol patterns?","Are controls (photocells, timers, BMS) set to ensure consistent coverage during hours of darkness?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Perform nighttime walk-throughs to map dark spots and adjust fixture location, aiming, and output.","Provide consistent, uniform lighting at building entries, pedestrian paths, and parking areas.","Coordinate fixture placement and light levels with camera locations to avoid glare and backlighting.","Implement clear procedures and SLAs for reporting and fixing lighting outages.","Trim vegetation and relocate obstructions that block light distribution."]
standards_references: ["IES (Illuminating Engineering Society) recommendations for security and area lighting","CPTED guidance on lighting and natural surveillance","CISA facility security lighting resources"]
psa_notes: Night visits are non-negotiable. Paper lighting plans and daytime inspections routinely miss the real problems that show up after dark.

name: Locking Hardware
overview: Locking hardware provides physical resistance to unauthorized entry and ensures secure closures for doors, gates, and controlled spaces.
indicators_of_risk: ["Damaged or misaligned strike plates","Doors not fully latching or easy to manipulate","Incompatible lock types for risk level","Classroom doors lacking proper lockdown capability","Doors routinely propped open"]
common_failures: ["Using low-security locks on sensitive rooms","Door closers not maintained, causing incomplete latching","Failure to comply with egress requirements for magnetic locks","No testing of locks during inspections"]
assessment_questions: ["Do all exterior and interior doors latch securely?","Are locks appropriate for the area’s risk profile?","Are classroom or office doors capable of emergency lockdown?","Are doors ever propped open and why?","Is locking hardware included in preventive maintenance?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Install reinforced strike plates and high-security locks.","Ensure closers are functional and adjusted properly.","Use compliant egress hardware on magnetically locked doors.","Implement a no-prop door policy with monitoring.","Conduct periodic lock inspections and adjustments."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 101 Life Safety Code","BHMA/ANSI Lock Standards"]
psa_notes: Poor locking hardware often represents systemic maintenance issues. Verify latching physically, not visually.

name: ICS/NIMS Integration
overview: ICS/NIMS integration ensures that a facility’s emergency response structure aligns with the Incident Command System and the National Incident Management System, enabling seamless coordination with public safety agencies.
indicators_of_risk: ["Staff unfamiliar with ICS roles such as Incident Commander or Public Information Officer","Emergency plans not aligned with ICS terminology or structure","During incidents, decisions made chaotically without defined authority","No unified command process for multi-agency response","After-action reviews reveal confusion over communication chains or responsibility"]
common_failures: ["Treating ICS as a government-only framework with no facility application","Assigning ICS roles informally with no training","Failing to integrate ICS concepts into emergency drills","Plans written using internal organization charts rather than ICS structure","Not coordinating with local responders on unified command"]
assessment_questions: ["How does the facility align its emergency organization with ICS/NIMS?","Are staff assigned facility-level ICS roles and trained in those roles?","Have ICS principles been tested in drills or table-top exercises?","Is there a process for establishing unified command with responders?","Do emergency plans use ICS terminology for clarity and compatibility?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Define facility-level ICS roles and train staff accordingly.","Align emergency action plans with ICS/NIMS structure and terminology.","Practice ICS-based decision-making during drills and exercises.","Engage local responders in unified command planning and exercises.","Use ICS forms and documentation for major incidents and after-actions."]
standards_references: ["FEMA ICS/NIMS doctrine","HSEEP exercise design guidelines"]
psa_notes: Ask staff who the Incident Commander would be for a real event. If no one knows, ICS is only on paper.

name: Redundancy / Backup Systems
overview: Redundancy and backup systems protect critical security functions from single points of failure, ensuring continued operation during outages, disasters, or technical failures.
indicators_of_risk: ["Security systems (VSS, access control, alarms) with no UPS or generator backup","Single network switch or server supporting all security systems","No plan for communications if radios, phones, or internet fail","Critical alarms silenced or ignored during power fluctuations","No periodic testing of backup systems under load"]
common_failures: ["Backup systems installed but never tested","UPS batteries expired or unable to sustain critical loads","Redundancy provided for IT systems but not for physical security technology","Reliance on cloud systems without offline contingency plans"]
assessment_questions: ["Which security functions must remain operational during a power or network outage?","What backup power supports critical systems, and for how long?","Do you test UPS, generators, and failover systems regularly?","What is the fallback for communications if phones or radios fail?","Are security systems isolated from IT single points of failure?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Provide UPS and generator backup for critical security systems.","Test backup power systems under realistic conditions.","Segment security networks to avoid IT outages taking down security operations.","Develop offline procedures for access control and communications.","Document redundancy architecture and maintain accurate diagrams."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 111 – stored electrical energy systems","CISA physical security resilience guidance","NIST continuity principles where applicable"]
psa_notes: Ask to see UPSes, test logs, and network diagrams. Declared redundancy often fails under real stress unless regularly validated.

name: Evacuation Procedures
overview: Evacuation procedures describe how occupants safely and rapidly leave hazardous areas or the entire facility during fires, chemical releases, bomb threats, and other emergencies. They must be clear, practiced, and tailored to the site.
indicators_of_risk: ["Staff or occupants unsure of primary and secondary evacuation routes","No accommodations in procedures for people with disabilities or mobility limitations","Evacuation routes blocked by storage, furniture, or locked doors","No accounting process at assembly/muster areas","Drills conducted rarely, only for compliance, or not reflective of real risks"]
common_failures: ["Using generic, template evacuation plans not aligned to actual layout","Treating evacuation as purely a fire issue, ignoring other scenarios","Failing to coordinate with local fire and emergency services","Not updating procedures after renovations or occupancy changes","Drills scheduled only when most convenient, not under realistic conditions"]
assessment_questions: ["What hazards trigger full or partial evacuation, and who decides?","What are the primary and alternate routes and exits for each major area?","How do you account for all occupants once evacuated?","How are people with mobility, sensory, or cognitive limitations assisted?","How often are evacuation drills conducted, and how are lessons learned captured?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Map evacuation routes and exits clearly and ensure signage is visible and maintained.","Develop hazard-specific evacuation guidance (fire, chemical release, bomb threat, etc.).","Integrate support procedures for individuals who need assistance evacuating.","Coordinate plans and drills with local fire and emergency responders.","Conduct regular, evaluated drills and update procedures based on after-action findings."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 101 Life Safety Code – means of egress and evacuation provisions","OSHA 29 CFR 1910.38 – Emergency Action Plans","FEMA and CISA emergency planning guidance for facilities"]
psa_notes: Ask random staff how they would evacuate and where they would go. Their answer is the real procedure; the written plan is just theory until it matches that behavior.

name: Biometric Access
overview: Biometric access systems use physical characteristics such as fingerprints, facial recognition, or iris scans to authenticate users. They provide strong identity verification but require strict enrollment, maintenance, and privacy safeguards.
indicators_of_risk: ["Biometric readers malfunctioning or frequently offline","Enrollment process inconsistent or poorly documented","Users able to bypass biometrics with override credentials","Cameras or sensors misaligned, dirty, or obstructed","No policy governing biometric data retention or privacy"]
common_failures: ["Failing to maintain sensors, causing false rejects or accepts","Using biometrics without a backup authentication method for outages","Inadequate training leading to improper use or long queues","Storing biometric data insecurely or without retention policies"]
assessment_questions: ["Which biometric modalities are used (fingerprint, facial, iris)?","How is biometric data stored, protected, and retained?","Is enrollment standardized and verified by trained personnel?","What backup methods exist during outages or sensor failures?","Are biometric readers maintained and cleaned regularly?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Establish privacy and retention policies aligned with best practices.","Regularly clean, calibrate, and test biometric readers.","Use multi-factor authentication where appropriate.","Document and secure biometric data with encryption.","Train staff on proper positioning and reader use."]
standards_references: ["NIST Biometric Application Guidelines","ASIS Access Control Standard"]
psa_notes: Biometric reliability varies by environment. High humidity, dirt, or glare often degrade performance — test sensors live on-site.

name: Crisis Management
overview: Crisis management establishes the decision-making structure and procedures a facility uses before, during, and after emergencies. Its effectiveness determines how quickly an incident transitions from uncontrolled to managed.
indicators_of_risk: ["Staff unclear who is in charge during emergencies","No documented crisis management plan or it is outdated","Conflicting procedures between departments or roles","Leaders are unaware of their crisis responsibilities","Exercises limited to fire drills with no scenario variety"]
common_failures: ["No activation criteria for transitioning from normal operations to crisis mode","No pre-identified command staff or alternates","Plans written for compliance rather than actual facility operations","No after-action reviews following drills or incidents"]
assessment_questions: ["How is a crisis declared, and who has authority to do so?","Which roles make up your crisis leadership team, and who are their alternates?","Walk me through your last major incident or drill. What changed afterward?","How are decisions communicated across the facility during a crisis?","Do you integrate external agencies into your planning?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Formalize a crisis leadership structure with primary and alternate roles.","Develop activation triggers for emergency response escalation.","Conduct multi-scenario exercises at least annually with documented AARs.","Align crisis plans with local public safety partners.","Integrate mass notification and communications pathways with crisis procedures."]
standards_references: ["FEMA Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101","CISA School Security Guide – Emergency Planning","NIMS/ICS doctrine for command structure"]
psa_notes: PSAs should assess whether staff can confidently explain who leads during a crisis and how decisions are made. A binder no one understands is not a crisis plan.

name: Exterior Wall Reinforcement
overview: Exterior wall reinforcement increases the ability of building envelopes to resist forced entry, small-arms fire, blast, and severe weather. It focuses on materials, connections, and continuity so walls perform as intended under attack or hazard.
indicators_of_risk: ["Critical occupied spaces protected only by lightweight stud-and-gypsum exterior walls","Large openings, mechanical louvers, or weak infill panels at ground level near public approach","Visible cracking, spalling, or corrosion in structural or masonry elements","Retrofits added to walls (pipes, raceways, penetrations) with no review of structural impact","No documentation of exterior wall ratings for blast, ballistic, or impact threats where such risks exist"]
common_failures: ["Upgrading doors and glazing while leaving adjacent walls easily breachable","Adding heavy cladding or equipment without verifying wall capacity or anchorage","Using non-reinforced masonry or lightweight panels in high-risk exposure zones","Allowing unprotected service penetrations at ground level that can be quickly exploited","Treating exterior walls as a single-threat issue (e.g., only hurricane) instead of multi-hazard"]
assessment_questions: ["Which exterior walls protect your most critical spaces (command, emergency operations, high-occupancy areas)?","What materials and reinforcement details are used in those walls?","Have walls near public approaches been evaluated for forced entry, ballistic, or blast resistance as appropriate?","Are there weak points such as louvers, access hatches, or penetrations that undermine overall wall performance?","Have structural or security engineers reviewed exterior wall conditions during major projects?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Prioritize reinforcement of walls protecting life-safety and mission-critical spaces.","Use reinforced masonry, concrete, or engineered wall systems where higher resistance is required.","Harden or relocate louvers, hatches, and large penetrations to reduce easy breach points.","Coordinate exterior wall upgrades with door, glazing, and roof anchorage improvements to maintain continuity.","Document as-built wall construction and incorporate it into future security and resilience planning."]
standards_references: ["FEMA 426/430 – guidance on building envelope hardening","DoD UFC protective design criteria for exterior walls","CISA building hardening and protective design resources"]
psa_notes: When doors and windows are strong but walls are weak, attackers will simply choose the path of least resistance. PSAs should look for those weak wall segments close to public approach.

name: Key Logs / Accountability
overview: Key logs and accountability systems ensure mechanical keys are issued, tracked, returned, and inventoried. They provide chain-of-custody and reduce the risk of unauthorized duplication or misuse.
indicators_of_risk: ["Key issuance not recorded or only tracked verbally","Log sheets missing dates, signatures, or purpose of checkout","Master keys issued without justification or supervisor approval","Keys unreturned after staff departure or contractor demobilization","No audit or reconciliation between physical keys and lock cores"]
common_failures: ["Maintaining logs that no one actually updates or enforces","Issuing keys to entire teams instead of specific individuals","Failing to document lost keys or initiate rekeying","Using paper logs with no integrity controls or backups","Not performing periodic audits of key inventory"]
assessment_questions: ["How do you record the issuance and return of all mechanical keys?","Who approves issuance of master or high-security keys?","How often are key logs audited for accuracy?","What is your process if a key is lost or not returned?","Do logs include purpose, time, and responsible individual?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Implement key control logs with signatures, time/date stamps, and justification.","Perform quarterly audits comparing logs to physical key inventory.","Restrict master key issuance to essential personnel with supervisor approval.","Document and enforce procedures for lost keys including rekeying.","Digitize logs or use electronic key cabinets to improve accountability."]
standards_references: ["ASIS physical key management guidance","NFPA 730 – key control provisions"]
psa_notes: Request the last 90 days of logs. If signatures are missing or keys show as “still out,” accountability is broken.

name: Resilience Planning
overview: Resilience planning prepares a facility to absorb, adapt to, and recover from disruptions, including natural hazards, security incidents, system failures, and long-term operational impacts.
indicators_of_risk: ["No continuity plan addressing critical functions or services","No redundancy for critical infrastructure (power, communications, access control)","No after-action review process following disruptions or near-misses","Leadership unaware of recovery priorities or dependencies","Plans not aligned with local emergency management or utility providers"]
common_failures: ["Resilience efforts focused only on IT and not on physical operations","Plans written once and not updated following expansions or reorganizations","No drills or exercises to validate recovery capabilities","Inadequate backup power for security-critical systems"]
assessment_questions: ["Which functions are critical to continued operations during a disruption?","How long can operations continue without power or communications?","Do you have documented continuity or resilience plans?","How have past disruptions informed current planning?","Have you coordinated plans with local emergency management or utilities?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Identify critical functions and map supporting infrastructure and personnel.","Develop continuity-of-operations plans with clear recovery priorities.","Ensure redundancy for power, communications, and access control systems.","Conduct resilience-focused exercises and update plans accordingly.","Coordinate resilience planning with local emergency management agencies."]
standards_references: ["FEMA Continuity Guidance Circular","CISA infrastructure resilience best practices","ISO 22301 – business continuity management principles"]
psa_notes: Resilience is proven by how an organization handles real disruptions. PSAs should review incident history and look for improvement over time.

name: Analytics / Behavior Detection
overview: Video analytics and behavior detection systems use algorithms to identify unusual activity such as loitering, perimeter breaches, object abandonment, or crowd anomalies. Their effectiveness depends on proper configuration, calibration, and operator understanding.
indicators_of_risk: ["Analytics generating excessive false alarms, causing operator fatigue","Critical behaviors not configured or monitored","Cameras not positioned to support analytic detection zones","No one reviewing analytic alerts or validating results","System performance degrading with no retraining or recalibration"]
common_failures: ["Treating analytics as a “set and forget” feature","Improper camera angles leading to missed detections","Overly sensitive settings causing nuisance alerts","Operators ignoring analytic panels entirely","Failure to match analytic types with camera field of view"]
assessment_questions: ["Which analytics are enabled and for what purpose?","Are alerts reviewed and validated by operators?","Are detection zones properly calibrated and updated?","Do camera positions support the analytic functions?","Are false alarm rates tracked and managed?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Recalibrate detection zones regularly.","Train operators on interpreting and responding to analytic alerts.","Position cameras specifically for analytics (height, angle, field).","Tune sensitivity to reduce false positives.","Integrate analytics into broader alarm workflows."]
standards_references: ["SIA Video Analytics Performance Standards","DHS Video Analytics Best Practices"]
psa_notes: Ask operators whether they trust analytic alerts; this reveals real-world performance better than vendor demos.

name: Electric Strikes / Mag Locks
overview: Electric strikes and magnetic locks secure doors by controlling whether they can open under electric or magnetic force. Their reliability depends on proper installation, fail-safe vs. fail-secure configuration, and integration with fire/life-safety systems.
indicators_of_risk: ["Incorrect fail-safe/fail-secure configuration for door purpose","Emergency egress paths blocked due to improper mag lock release wiring","Doors not releasing on fire alarm activation","Excessive door movement weakening mag lock alignment","Strikes installed on incompatible door frames or hardware"]
common_failures: ["Mag locks requiring manual push-to-exit buttons without proper timing (code violation)","No fire alarm tie-in, creating life safety hazards","Installing mag locks on doors with inadequate structural support","Doors slamming or misaligned, reducing holding force"]
assessment_questions: ["Are mag locks tied into the fire alarm system?","Is the fail-safe/fail-secure mode appropriate for the risk?","Do doors release properly during power outages or emergency tests?","Is hardware aligned and tested regularly?","Are egress requirements met according to local code?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Verify life-safety compliance through regular release testing.","Ensure proper fail-safe/fail-secure configurations.","Align and reinforce doors supporting mag locks.","Integrate locks with fire alarm and emergency egress requirements.","Use bonded sensors to confirm door secure/door open states."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 101 Life Safety Code – Door Hardware Requirements","DHI Door and Hardware Standards"]
psa_notes: Improper mag lock installation is one of the most common life-safety violations encountered. Perform functional testing during assessment.

name: Wayfinding
overview: Wayfinding systems use signage, maps, color cues, lighting, and architectural guidance to direct occupants through a facility. Effective wayfinding reduces confusion during emergencies, improves circulation, and supports rapid response by staff and responders.
indicators_of_risk: ["Inconsistent or missing directional signage for exits, stairwells, and key areas","Visitors regularly asking staff for directions, indicating unclear layout","Emergency routes not marked or contradicting posted maps","Signage obscured by decorations, furniture, or renovations","Color coding or zone labeling present in some areas but not others"]
common_failures: ["Relying on ad hoc signage added over years without standardization","Using overly complex maps or text-heavy signs that cannot be understood under stress","Failing to maintain signage during construction or remodel phases","Not coordinating wayfinding design with emergency evacuation routes and muster points","Ignoring accessibility and multilingual needs for diverse occupants"]
assessment_questions: ["Can a first-time visitor navigate to key locations without assistance?","Do signs align with emergency evacuation routes and assembly areas?","Is wayfinding consistent across buildings, floors, or wings?","Has signage been evaluated during drills for clarity and speed?","Are color coding, symbols, or zone identifiers used to simplify navigation?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Develop a standardized wayfinding system with consistent design, fonts, and symbols.","Ensure all emergency egress paths, stairwells, and refuge areas are clearly marked.","Use color coding and simple iconography to guide unfamiliar occupants quickly.","Audit signage during drills and update confusing or contradictory elements.","Maintain visibility by placing signage above obstructions and ensuring adequate lighting."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 101 – Life Safety Code requirements for egress signage","ADA Standards for Accessible Design – signage requirements","CPTED principles regarding visibility and intuitive navigation"]
psa_notes: Walk the facility as if you have never been there. If you get lost or hesitate at intersections, occupants will too—especially during emergencies.

name: Shelter-in-Place
overview: Shelter-in-place procedures keep occupants indoors and protected from external hazards such as severe weather, airborne contaminants, or community violence when evacuation is unsafe or impractical.
indicators_of_risk: ["Staff and students unsure which rooms or areas are designated for shelter-in-place","Designated shelter areas with large unprotected exterior windows for weather-related events","Shelter spaces too small for the number of people expected to use them","No plan to communicate “all clear” and controlled release from shelter","No consideration for individuals with mobility, medical, or sensory needs"]
common_failures: ["Shelter-in-place plans copied from templates without site-specific analysis","Shelter drilling avoided or done only as a classroom discussion instead of practice","Rooms designated on paper but cluttered, locked, or otherwise unusable in practice","No backup communications in shelter areas if primary systems fail"]
assessment_questions: ["Which rooms or areas are designated for shelter-in-place, and for which hazards?","How quickly can occupants reach those locations from typical occupied spaces?","How do you notify occupants to shelter-in-place and later issue an all-clear?","Do you have separate guidance for weather, chemical, and community-threat scenarios?","How are people with disabilities or special needs supported during shelter-in-place?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Select shelter spaces based on hazard type (interior, windowless rooms for severe weather; secure interior spaces for external violence).","Clearly mark shelter locations and train staff and students on routes.","Ensure shelter spaces remain accessible and free of obstructive storage.","Provide shelter areas with minimal supplies and redundant communication options.","Incorporate shelter-in-place drills into the overall exercise program and adjust based on after-action findings."]
standards_references: ["FEMA guidance on tornado, severe weather, and safe rooms","CISA school and facility shelter-in-place best practices","NFPA 101 – provisions related to interior refuge and safe areas where applicable"]
psa_notes: PSAs should physically inspect declared shelter spaces. If they are glass-heavy, cluttered, unmarked, or unknown to staff, the shelter plan exists only on paper.

name: Panic / Duress Buttons
overview: Panic and duress buttons provide a discreet means for staff to summon help when threatened, assaulted, or witnessing a rapidly developing security incident. They are common at reception desks, cash handling points, clinics, and isolated work areas.
indicators_of_risk: ["Frontline staff in public-facing roles reporting threats or aggression with no duress capability","Existing buttons whose activation behavior (who responds, how fast) is unclear to staff","Buttons blocked, damaged, or difficult to access from normal working posture","No testing program to verify signaling, response times, and monitoring paths","Contract guards or dispatch unaware of all installed duress locations"]
common_failures: ["Installing duress buttons to satisfy a design requirement and never training staff on use","Buttons wired only to local tones or lights that no one monitors consistently","Hidden placement that makes activation awkward or impossible under stress","No documented response protocol or priority for duress alarms","Testing skipped due to fear of “false police dispatches,” leading to unknown readiness"]
assessment_questions: ["Where are panic/duress buttons installed and how were locations selected?","Who receives duress alarms and what is the expected response timeline?","Are staff trained on when and how to activate duress, and what happens afterward?","How often are duress devices function-tested and are tests documented?","Are duress alarms integrated with VSS call-up, radio notifications, or access control actions?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Map all existing duress devices and validate that high-risk posts are covered.","Develop clear response protocols for duress activations, including law enforcement notification criteria.","Train staff on appropriate use, including expectations about false alarms during testing.","Conduct regular, documented testing of duress buttons and communication paths.","Integrate duress alarms with camera call-up and radio/dispatch workflows where feasible."]
standards_references: ["UL 636 – Holdup Alarm Units and Systems","ASIS guidelines for workplace violence prevention and response","CISA soft target and crowded places security resources"]
psa_notes: Do not just ask if duress exists—have staff show you where the buttons are and explain what happens when they press them. Hesitation or conflicting answers are red flags.

name: Video Wall / Display Systems
overview: Video wall and display systems present multiple camera feeds, alarms, and situational awareness tools for security operators. Their value depends on ergonomic layout, data prioritization, and integration with monitoring workflows.
indicators_of_risk: ["Operators manually cycling through cameras instead of automated display rules","Displays showing irrelevant or unused feeds","Poor ergonomics causing operator fatigue","Critical alarms not prioritized or highlighted on displays","Video wall systems outdated or not synchronized with VMS"]
common_failures: ["Crowding too many cameras on a single display","Ignoring alarm-triggered auto-callup features","Poor lighting or screen brightness reducing visibility","Outdated hardware causing lag or display failure","Improper monitoring workflows leading to missed events"]
assessment_questions: ["Are video walls configured to highlight critical alarms?","Do operators rely on the video wall for real monitoring?","Are camera layouts organized by zones or risk areas?","Is the display hardware maintained and tested?","Are lighting and ergonomics supportive of long-duration monitoring?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Implement alarm-based auto-display rules.","Organize video feeds logically by zones or risk tiers.","Upgrade outdated display hardware for clarity and reliability.","Optimize ergonomic layout for operator comfort.","Regularly audit display content for relevance."]
standards_references: ["ASIS Security Operations Center Guidelines","SIA Video Monitoring Best Practices"]
psa_notes: If operators rely more on spot monitors or ad-hoc screens than the video wall, the layout is likely misconfigured or overloaded.

name: Security Documentation
overview: Security documentation includes plans, procedures, post orders, maps, drawings, maintenance records, and logs that support consistent, informed security operations and assessments.
indicators_of_risk: ["No central repository for security-related documents","Floor plans, camera maps, or access control schematics outdated or missing","Post orders handwritten, inconsistent, or contradicted by other documents","Incident logs incomplete, missing, or stored informally (e.g., notebooks)","No document control practices—staff unsure which version is current"]
common_failures: ["Relying on institutional memory instead of maintaining formal records","Documentation created for one project and never updated again","Critical documentation stored in a single vulnerable location","No process for providing key documentation to first responders during an incident"]
assessment_questions: ["Where do you store your key security documents and who has access?","How do you maintain and update floor plans, camera layouts, and device inventories?","Do you keep logs of incidents, alarms, or maintenance? How are they used?","How do you ensure staff are using the latest versions of procedures and post orders?","Can you quickly provide responders with relevant documentation during an incident?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Establish a controlled repository (digital and/or physical) for security documentation.","Implement version control and review cycles for critical documents.","Keep accurate, up-to-date maps of cameras, access control devices, and critical infrastructure.","Maintain logs for incidents, alarms, and maintenance and review them regularly for trends.","Prepare a responder packet or digital set that can be quickly shared during an incident."]
standards_references: ["CISA facility security planning guidance","NFPA and building code requirements for life-safety documentation","General ISO-style documentation control practices adapted to security"]
psa_notes: Good documentation makes patterns and problems visible. PSAs should look for consistency, currency, and practical usability, not just volume of paperwork.

name: Interior Cameras
overview: Interior cameras provide video coverage of corridors, entries, lobbies, stairwells, and critical rooms inside the facility. They support deterrence, incident reconstruction, and verification of alarms and staff reports.
indicators_of_risk: ["Key corridors, stairwells, or interior chokepoints with no camera coverage","Lobby or reception areas lacking identification-quality views of faces","High-value rooms (pharmacy, cash office, IT, records) with no interior cameras","Camera views blocked by decorations, signs, or temporary storage","Staff unsure who, if anyone, monitors interior cameras or reviews footage"]
common_failures: ["Relying only on exterior cameras while ignoring interior movement and escalation paths","Placing cameras too high or at poor angles for facial identification","Allowing interior layouts, partitions, or furniture to change without reassessing coverage","Treating cameras as “nice to have” and never checking image quality or retention","Using cameras primarily for HR/discipline issues rather than security risk management"]
assessment_questions: ["Which interior spaces are considered most critical to monitor and why?","Do cameras capture clear facial images at main interior entries and reception points?","How are blind spots in corridors, stairwells, and elevator lobbies identified and mitigated?","Who is responsible for routinely checking camera health, image quality, and retention?","Are interior camera locations documented and tied to floor plans or SOC displays?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Prioritize coverage of lobbies, main corridors, stairwells, and high-value rooms.","Position cameras at heights and angles that support facial identification and activity recognition.","Reassess coverage after remodels, furniture changes, or new partitions.","Establish routine inspections for focus, field-of-view, cleanliness, and recording status.","Integrate interior cameras with access control and intrusion events for rapid verification."]
standards_references: ["IEC 62676 – Video surveillance systems for security applications","NFPA 730 – Guide for Premises Security (video surveillance sections)","CISA facility security guidance on interior monitoring"]
psa_notes: Walk interior circulation routes and ask, “If an incident started here, could we see it well enough to act and later prove what happened?” Weak answers indicate a gap regardless of hardware brand.

name: Law Enforcement Liaison
overview: Law enforcement liaison procedures define how a facility exchanges threat information, requests assistance, and cooperates during emergencies. Strong relationships reduce response time and improve situational awareness.
indicators_of_risk: ["Facility leadership has no established point of contact in law enforcement","Law enforcement unfamiliar with facility layout or operations","No periodic communication or joint meetings","Facility uncertain how or when to report suspicious activities","Law enforcement not included in major drills"]
common_failures: ["Lack of pre-incident coordination causing confusion during emergencies","Outdated or incorrect contact lists for law enforcement","Failure to report concerning behaviors or threats promptly"]
assessment_questions: ["Who is your primary and alternate contact in local law enforcement?","How often do you engage with them to discuss threats or trends?","Have they toured the facility or reviewed floor plans?","Are they involved in your emergency drills or planning processes?","How do you report suspicious behavior or threats?"]
