mitigation_guidance: ["Establish formal primary and alternate law enforcement contacts.","Schedule recurring coordination meetings, especially for high-risk sites.","Provide law enforcement with updated floor plans and facility access information.","Include law enforcement in major exercises and scenario planning.","Implement a clear threat reporting procedure for staff."]
standards_references: ["Nationwide SAR Initiative (NSI) guidance","CISA Infrastructure Security guidance","Fusion Center Liaison Officer (FLO) best practices"]
psa_notes: Facilities with strong pre-existing relationships tend to handle crises faster and with fewer casualties. PSAs should probe specifically for personal rather than generic agency-to-facility connections.

name: ISAC / ISAOs
overview: Information Sharing and Analysis Centers (ISACs) and Information Sharing and Analysis Organizations (ISAOs) provide sector-focused threat intelligence, best practices, and alerts. Effective engagement helps facilities anticipate and prepare for emerging risks.
indicators_of_risk: ["Facility is unaware of or not enrolled in any relevant ISAC/ISAO","ISAC bulletins received but never reviewed or acted upon","No internal process to route ISAC/ISAO information to decision-makers","Training and exercises not informed by sector threat trends","Leadership cannot name the primary ISAC/ISAO for their sector"]
common_failures: ["Treating ISAC/ISAO membership as a check-the-box credential","Forwarding bulletins indiscriminately instead of prioritizing actionable content","No designated point-of-contact responsible for ISAC/ISAO engagement","Failure to contribute local observations or incidents back to the community","Allowing subscription or access credentials to lapse unnoticed"]
assessment_questions: ["Are you a member of, or connected to, any sector ISAC or ISAO?","Who receives ISAC/ISAO bulletins and what do they do with them?","Can you give examples of changes made based on ISAC/ISAO information?","Do you ever provide incident or near-miss information back to the ISAC/ISAO?","How is relevant intelligence shared with front-line staff or supervisors?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Identify the primary ISAC/ISAO(s) for your sector and enroll appropriate points-of-contact.","Assign a responsible individual or team to triage and act on shared information.","Develop an internal distribution process so relevant bulletins inform risk assessments, training, and procedures.","Encourage two-way sharing by reporting significant incidents or suspicious activity as appropriate.","Periodically review membership, access, and usage to ensure value is being realized."]
standards_references: ["DHS guidance on ISAC/ISAO participation","Sector-specific security and resilience frameworks referencing ISACs"]
psa_notes: Ask for concrete examples: “Show me one change you made last year based on an ISAC/ISAO product.” If they cannot, the relationship is passive, not operational.

name: Window Film
overview: Security window film reinforces existing glazing to reduce shattering and delay forced entry. While not a substitute for true impact-resistant glass, film improves fragmentation control and provides limited forced-entry resistance.
indicators_of_risk: ["Film installed without proper edge anchoring systems","Bubbling, peeling, or UV-damaged film reducing effectiveness","Film used in place of appropriate glazing for high-risk locations","No certification or rating information for installed film","Film installed only on lower panes while upper panes remain vulnerable"]
common_failures: ["Assuming film makes standard glass fully impact-resistant","Skipping anchoring systems that prevent the glass from dislodging","Improper installation causing voids, weak points, or premature failure","Not testing or documenting film performance during renovations","Relying on film for high-threat conditions (forced entry, blast) without engineering support"]
assessment_questions: ["What type of film was installed and does it include edge anchoring?","Who installed the film and were they certified?","Is film applied to all glazing within reach or just selected panes?","Are maintenance and inspection procedures documented?","Was film part of a risk-informed glazing strategy?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Use properly anchored, professionally installed film for ground-level glazing.","Verify product performance claims against recognized standards.","Inspect annually for peeling, bubbling, or UV degradation.","Apply film consistently across vulnerable glass areas.","Combine film with additional hardening measures for higher-risk zones."]
standards_references: ["GSA/DoD glazing hazard mitigation guidelines","ASTM standards for glazing and safety film performance"]
psa_notes: Film without anchoring is cosmetic protection only. Ask specifically about anchoring and installation method.

name: Perimeter Signage
overview: Perimeter signage communicates boundaries, restricted areas, hazards, and entry instructions. Effective signage supports deterrence, legal enforceability, and clarity for visitors and staff.
indicators_of_risk: ["No signage indicating restricted or controlled areas","Signs faded, damaged, or not visible at night","Conflicting or unclear instructions for visitors or drivers","Sign placement too high, too low, or obscured by vegetation","Lack of multilingual or symbol-based signage where appropriate"]
common_failures: ["Assuming fencing alone provides sufficient boundary communication","Installing signs sporadically rather than following a perimeter plan","Using small or non-reflective signage not visible from approach distances","Failing to update signage after process, route, or risk changes"]
assessment_questions: ["Do perimeter signs clearly indicate restricted areas and entry points?","Are signs reflective and visible under nighttime conditions?","Is signage consistent in design, language, and placement?","Do signs support legal enforcement of trespassing or restricted access?","Is vegetation maintained to preserve sign visibility?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Develop a standardized signage plan for all perimeter boundaries.","Use reflective, durable, weather-resistant materials.","Include multilingual or symbol-based warnings where needed.","Place signage at consistent intervals along controlled edges.","Ensure signage aligns with access control and response procedures."]
standards_references: ["CPTED boundary definition principles","CISA perimeter deterrence guidelines"]
psa_notes: Signage is often overlooked but critical. Without clear warnings and instructions, enforcement becomes difficult—even with good physical barriers.

name: Master Key Management
overview: Master key management ensures physical keys, including master and sub-master keys, are controlled, tracked, and secured to prevent unauthorized access.
indicators_of_risk: ["No log of who holds master keys","Master keys stored in unlocked or unsecured locations","Contractors provided with unrestricted key access","Lost keys not reported or mitigated","No annual key audit or inventory"]
common_failures: ["Over-issuing master keys to convenience users","No policy governing key issuance or return","Failure to rekey after key loss or employee separation","Lack of secure storage for key boxes"]
assessment_questions: ["Who controls and maintains the key registry?","How many master keys exist and who holds them?","What is the process for reporting a lost key?","Are contractors allowed key access without oversight?","Are high-security keys used for sensitive areas?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Enforce strict issuance and return procedures.","Use secure electronic key cabinets with audit trails.","Conduct annual key audits.","Rekey immediately if a master key is lost.","Restrict contractor access to supervised or limited-use keys."]
standards_references: ["ISC Physical Security Criteria","ASIS Key Control Guidance"]
psa_notes: Master key mismanagement can undermine every other physical security measure. Confirm logs and storage with physical inspection.

name: IP Cameras
overview: IP cameras provide network-connected video coverage for interior and exterior areas. They support deterrence, detection, and post-incident investigation when properly placed, configured, and monitored.
indicators_of_risk: ["Obvious coverage gaps at primary entries, parking areas, or high-risk interior spaces","Cameras pointed at walls, bright windows, or areas with severe backlighting","No visible monitoring location or staff unable to state who watches cameras and when","Inconsistent retention—staff unsure how long video is stored or how to retrieve it","Network equipment or NVRs located in unsecured or environmentally poor spaces"]
common_failures: ["Systems installed as one-time projects with no planned maintenance or lifecycle replacement","Critical cameras offline for extended periods without detection","Retention periods too short to support investigations (days instead of weeks)","No documented process for exporting and preserving video for law enforcement","VSS not integrated with intrusion or access control for event-triggered review"]
assessment_questions: ["Which areas do you consider most critical to monitor, and how were camera locations selected?","How long is video retained, and who has authority to export or review it?","Who is responsible for checking camera health, and how often is that done?","Where is video stored (NVR, cloud, hybrid), and how is that infrastructure protected?","Do you review video proactively (e.g., after alarms) or only after major incidents?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Conduct a camera coverage review focused on entries, circulation paths, and high-risk spaces.","Establish clear retention targets aligned with investigative needs and storage capacity.","Implement routine health checks for cameras, storage, and recording infrastructure.","Integrate VSS with intrusion and access control systems to streamline incident review.","Document and train staff on procedures for video export, chain of custody, and law enforcement support."]
standards_references: ["IEC 62676 – Video surveillance systems for use in security applications","CISA facility security guidance referencing surveillance","NFPA 730 – premises security (video surveillance sections)"]
psa_notes: PSAs should avoid getting lost in pixel counts and model numbers. The key question is: “If something happens here, can anyone see it in time to respond, and can you prove what happened later?” Weak answers indicate a vulnerability regardless of hardware brand.

name: Interior Doors
overview: Interior doors support compartmentalization, access control, and life-safety functions inside the facility. They define secure zones, restrict circulation, and slow adversary movement.
indicators_of_risk: ["Doors propped open or failing to latch properly","Unsecured doors providing bypass around access control points","Hollow-core or low-grade doors protecting sensitive spaces","Missing or disabled closers, latches, or strikes","Door hardware mismatched with intended security function"]
common_failures: ["Allowing convenience culture to override interior door policy","Installing residential-grade or hollow-core doors in critical areas","Neglecting closer maintenance leading to incomplete latching","Removing doors or hardware during renovations without security review","No record of periodic door inspections"]
assessment_questions: ["Do interior doors critical to security consistently latch?","Are sensitive rooms protected by appropriate door construction?","Are doors ever propped open, and if so, why?","Are closers, hinges, and strikes functioning properly?","Is there a routine inspection or maintenance schedule?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Upgrade hollow-core doors in sensitive areas to solid-core or rated options.","Ensure closers and latches operate reliably and consistently.","Install door-held-open alarms where propping is common.","Inspect hardware regularly and coordinate with facilities teams.","Integrate sensitive interior doors into access control systems where appropriate."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 80 – Fire Door Assembly Requirements","DHI hardware standards for interior door protection"]
psa_notes: Walk the building and gently pull every door—if any don’t latch, they’re useless from a security standpoint regardless of access control.

name: Paging Systems
overview: Paging systems provide targeted or facility-wide audio communication, often used in healthcare, retail, industrial, and education settings. Their emergency utility depends on clarity, zoning, and response integration.
indicators_of_risk: ["Paging zones misconfigured or overlapping causing confusion","Paging volume insufficient in high-noise or large open areas","Outdated hardware with inconsistent audio quality","Staff unaware of paging codes or procedures","Paging system not included in emergency tests"]
common_failures: ["Using paging solely for routine announcements without emergency integration","No training on coded paging language (e.g., medical or threat codes)","Paging systems interfering with PA or mass notification clarity","System maintenance deferred leading to dead speakers or static","Paging not audible in exterior or remote parts of the facility"]
assessment_questions: ["How are paging zones configured and managed?","Are paging codes standardized and documented?","Can emergency messages override routine paging?","How often is the paging system tested?","Does the paging coverage include high-risk or remote areas?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Standardize paging codes and train all staff on their meaning.","Integrate paging with PA and mass notification systems.","Conduct regular intelligibility and coverage testing.","Replace or repair malfunctioning speakers or amplifiers.","Ensure emergency messages override all normal paging traffic."]
standards_references: ["NFPA 72 ECS guidance","Healthcare and education emergency paging best practices"]
psa_notes: Paging is only useful if every part of the facility hears it. Test stairwells, restrooms, warehouses, and parking structures.

name: Security Operations Center (SOC)
overview: A Security Operations Center (SOC) is the central hub for monitoring, communications, and decision-making for security events. SOC maturity is measured less by screens and more by procedures, staffing, and integration.
indicators_of_risk: ["SOC present but often unstaffed or staffed by untrained personnel","No clear SOPs for alarm handling, video review, or incident escalation","Multiple systems (access, VSS, intrusion) monitored separately with no integrated view","SOC environment noisy, distracting, or used for unrelated tasks","No formal shift handover process or event logging"]
common_failures: ["SOC built as a capital project with little investment in ongoing training and staffing","Operators overloaded with non-security responsibilities (answering main phone lines, general admin)","No regular review of SOC performance or incident handling metrics","SOC not involved in planning or debriefing of major events and drills"]
assessment_questions: ["Who staffs the SOC and what qualifications or training do they have?","Which systems are monitored there and how are alerts prioritized?","How are incidents documented, handed over between shifts, and reviewed?","What authority does the SOC have to initiate responses or notifications?","How is the SOC protected from power loss, network outages, or physical disruption?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Define SOC roles, responsibilities, and authority clearly.","Develop and train to SOC-specific SOPs for common incident types.","Integrate key systems into consolidated dashboards where feasible.","Implement formal shift handover and event logging processes.","Ensure the SOC space itself is secure, ergonomic, and designed for sustained operations."]
standards_references: ["CISA guidance on security operations and monitoring","ASIS Physical Security and SOC design best practices"]
psa_notes: A SOC is effective when operators can clearly describe how they detect, verify, communicate, and initiate response—PSAs should focus on those functions rather than the hardware alone.

name: Lockdown / Lockout Procedures
overview: Lockdown and lockout procedures provide structured steps to secure a facility or isolate threats, commonly used in schools, offices, and public venues.
indicators_of_risk: ["No written lockdown/lockout protocol","Staff unable to describe lockdown steps","No secondary communication method for updates","Rooms lacking the ability to secure from the inside","Inconsistent or unrealistic drill performance"]
common_failures: ["Confusing lockdown vs. lockout terminology","Failure to include visitors or contractors in procedures","No provisions for mobility-impaired occupants","Plans not coordinated with law enforcement"]
assessment_questions: ["Is there a written lockdown/lockout procedure?","Can rooms be secured from within during lockdown?","How often are drills conducted and evaluated?","How are communications handled during lockdowns?","Are procedures coordinated with local law enforcement?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Develop clear, scenario-based lockdown and lockout procedures.","Train all staff and conduct regular drills.","Ensure all rooms can be secured internally.","Coordinate with police to align terminology and response.","Document and evaluate drill performance for improvements."]
standards_references: ["DHS Active Shooter Guidelines","K-12 School Security Best Practices"]
psa_notes: Verify rooms truly secure from the inside. Walk through drill results and identify chokepoints or confusion points.

name: Structural Hardening
overview: Structural hardening enhances the building’s ability to resist forced entry, blast effects, seismic events, or deliberate protection failures by reinforcing walls, supports, doors, ceilings, and critical structural elements.
indicators_of_risk: ["Critical walls made of lightweight materials easily breached","Structural elements exposed or unprotected in public zones","No assessment of load-bearing impacts when modifying security features","Gaps between hardened and non-hardened elements creating bypass points","No engineering documentation supporting hardening decisions"]
common_failures: ["Applying point hardening (one room) without securing adjacent weak areas","Ignoring ceiling and plenum bypass routes","Installing heavy hardening materials without structural engineering review","Failing to coordinate between security, facilities, and engineering teams","Not maintaining hardening improvements during renovations"]
assessment_questions: ["Which spaces were selected for hardening and why?","Do structural upgrades align with the threat assessment?","Was an engineer involved in evaluating wall, ceiling, and floor strength?","How do hardened areas integrate with doors, glazing, and barriers?","Has hardening been maintained through renovations?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Deploy structural hardening based on a risk and threat-based engineering assessment.","Ensure all bypass routes (ceilings, adjacent rooms, weak walls) are addressed.","Use certified materials and consult engineers for installation.","Document all hardening work and update plans after modifications.","Integrate hardening with access control, surveillance, and emergency response design."]
standards_references: ["FEMA 426/431 – building hardening guidelines","DoD UFC protective design standards","CISA facility hardening recommendations"]
psa_notes: Always check ceilings and adjacent rooms. Most “hardened” rooms can be bypassed in under a minute due to overlooked weak points.

name: Threat Information Sharing
overview: Threat information sharing covers the timely exchange of relevant threat indicators, suspicious activity, and trend information between the facility, law enforcement, fusion centers, ISACs, and peer organizations.
indicators_of_risk: ["No defined process for reporting suspicious activity to external partners","Staff unsure when a concerning behavior rises to the level of external reporting","Facility rarely or never receives threat updates from law enforcement or fusion centers","No internal mechanism to capture and trend local threat-related reports","Sensitive information shared informally (e.g., text, personal email) with no control"]
common_failures: ["Over-reporting trivial issues while under-reporting credible concerns","Relying on informal personal relationships instead of structured channels","Lack of clear thresholds for when to notify law enforcement or fusion centers","Failure to protect personally identifiable or sensitive information during sharing","Not feeding external threat information back into training, drills, and mitigation planning"]
assessment_questions: ["How can staff report suspicious behavior or threats, and to whom?","When do you notify law enforcement, the fusion center, or your ISAC?","Do you have written guidance on what constitutes reportable suspicious activity?","How is threat information from external partners disseminated internally?","Can you provide examples where threat information changed security posture or procedures?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Develop clear reporting thresholds and simple procedures for staff to submit threat-related observations.","Establish structured channels with law enforcement, fusion centers, and sector partners.","Train staff on recognizing and reporting suspicious activity (SAR concepts).","Protect sensitive data by using approved, secured channels for external sharing.","Integrate threat information into risk assessments, training plans, and exercise design."]
standards_references: ["Nationwide Suspicious Activity Reporting (SAR) Initiative","Fusion center and CISA guidance on threat information sharing"]
psa_notes: Look for evidence of both inbound and outbound sharing—bulletins alone do not equal a threat information sharing program.

name: Fencing
overview: Fencing establishes a physical boundary and helps channel legitimate access through controlled points. It supports deterrence, delay, and clear demarcation between public and controlled space.
indicators_of_risk: ["Gaps under or around fence lines that allow easy passage","Fence terminals that end at climbable structures, trees, or buildings","Damaged, leaning, or cut fence sections that remain unrepaired","Uncontrolled pedestrian paths that bypass fenced perimeters","Lighting deficiencies along fence lines and access points"]
common_failures: ["Fencing installed without clear purpose, leaving gates or gaps effectively open","Poor integration between fence lines, gates, and vehicle barriers","No routine inspection or maintenance of fence integrity","Fence not extended to cover obvious approach routes or adjacent properties","Gates left open for convenience or blocked in the open position"]
assessment_questions: ["What is the intended security function of the fence (boundary, deterrence, delay)?","How often are fence lines and gates inspected for damage or tampering?","Are there known “informal” paths where people bypass the fence routinely?","How do you secure gates during and after business hours?","Is lighting adequate along the fence and at gates for detection and response?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Align fence design and placement with clear security objectives (boundary vs. delay).","Eliminate obvious bypass routes, including informal paths and adjacent structures.","Implement a scheduled inspection and repair program for fence lines and gates.","Ensure gates are secured when not in use and covered by lighting and surveillance.","Coordinate fencing with vehicle barriers, signage, and access control to support a coherent perimeter strategy."]
standards_references: ["CPTED principles on territorial reinforcement and boundary definition","DoD UFC and DHS perimeter security guidance where applicable","NFPA 730 – perimeter and boundary security"]
psa_notes: PSAs should walk the fence line instead of just observing from the facility side. The ease with which they can step over, go around, or exploit nearby structures is usually a direct indicator of whether the fence is a real control or just a visual boundary.

name: Guard Posts
overview: Guard posts are fixed positions where security personnel monitor activity, control access, and coordinate response. Their location, design, staffing, and procedures determine how effectively they influence behavior and detect threats.
indicators_of_risk: ["Guard posts located where guards have poor visibility of critical approaches or entries","Posts used primarily for administrative tasks rather than active observation","No clear post orders or inconsistent expectations between shifts","Guard posts unattended for long periods due to other assigned duties","Guard posts lacking communications, panic alarms, or protective features"]
common_failures: ["Treating guard posts as reception desks instead of security positions","Assigning guards additional tasks that pull them away from their post","Poorly designed posts that provide comfort but limited sightlines","No formal relief or handover procedures between guards","No metrics or supervision focused on guard post performance"]
assessment_questions: ["What is the primary mission of each guard post (deterrence, screening, monitoring)?","From this post, what can the guard actually see and control?","Are there written post orders and do guards know them without prompting?","How is the post staffed, relieved, and supervised across all shifts?","What communications, alarms, and protective features does the post have?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Align guard post placement with critical sightlines, chokepoints, and entry routes.","Develop clear, role-based post orders and review them with all guards.","Ensure posts are continuously staffed or covered by defined relief procedures.","Equip posts with reliable communications, panic/duress options, and situational awareness tools.","Periodically evaluate guard post effectiveness through inspections and drills."]
standards_references: ["ASIS Private Security Officer and Guard Force Management guidelines","CISA facility security guidance on guard force deployment"]
psa_notes: Stand at the guard post and watch for a few minutes. If the guard is buried in paperwork, answering phones, or watching nothing important, the post is a sunk cost instead of a control.

name: Credential / Badge Systems
overview: Credential systems govern issuance, design, and management of identification badges used for access control. Weak credential programs undermine all downstream security systems.
indicators_of_risk: ["Badges not visually distinguishing staff, contractors, and visitors","Badge issuance handled informally without identity verification","No process to recover badges when staff leave","Contractor badges identical to employee credentials","Access levels not tied to job function"]
common_failures: ["Badge templates not standardized or poorly controlled","Expired or unauthorized badges not removed from the system","Temporary badges reused without tracking","Photo IDs outdated or not enforced"]
assessment_questions: ["How do you verify the identity of new badge holders?","What is the process for deactivating badges when individuals leave?","How do you distinguish visitors, contractors, and temporary staff?","Are badge photos updated regularly?","Do supervisors approve access levels?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Standardize badge templates and enforce visual differentiation.","Implement strict badge recovery and deactivation procedures.","Tie badge access levels to HR/role changes.","Replace or update badge photos on a defined cycle."]
standards_references: ["FIPS 201 – identity credential standards","NIST SP 800-116 – credential authentication"]
psa_notes: PSAs should look for badges that all look alike. When visitors and contractors are indistinguishable from staff, risk increases dramatically.

name: Reunification Procedures
overview: Reunification procedures ensure safe, orderly, and accountable reunification of students, staff, or occupants with families or authorized parties after an emergency. Effective reunification reduces chaos, stress, and risk of misidentification.
indicators_of_risk: ["No written reunification plan or reliance on ad hoc decisions during incidents","Parents or guardians routinely arriving on scene during drills or minor events due to confusion","No controlled location for reunification separate from response operations","No verification process for authorized release of minors or vulnerable individuals","Inadequate communications to families during or after emergencies"]
common_failures: ["Reunification treated as an afterthought to evacuation or shelter-in-place","Plans that assume perfect data availability (rosters, contacts) during real incidents","Crowds of parents converging on entrances, complicating response efforts","Staff unprepared for emotional, medical, or custody-related complications","No integration with law enforcement or emergency management"]
assessment_questions: ["Where is your primary and alternate reunification location?","How do you verify that individuals picking up minors are authorized?","How do you communicate reunification instructions to families?","Who leads reunification operations and how are roles assigned?","How do you handle students or occupants with special needs during reunification?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Develop a step-by-step reunification plan with designated primary and backup sites.","Train staff on verification procedures and documentation workflows.","Use controlled entry/exit points and signage to manage family flow.","Coordinate plans with law enforcement and emergency management agencies.","Communicate reunification expectations to families during non-emergency times."]
standards_references: ["CISA K-12 School Security Guide – reunification considerations","I Love U Guys Foundation – Standard Reunification Method (widely adopted)","FEMA school and campus emergency operations planning guidance"]
psa_notes: A reunification plan is only real if staff know their roles and families know what to expect. PSAs should look at communications, verification, and crowd management — not just paperwork.

name: Safe Rooms
overview: Safe rooms are specially designed spaces intended to protect occupants from severe weather, violence, or other extreme hazards. They feature enhanced structural, access, and life-safety characteristics.
indicators_of_risk: ["Spaces labeled as “safe rooms” but lacking hardened construction or rated assemblies","Safe rooms with doors that do not reliably lock or latch under stress","No ventilation, lighting, or communication provision for extended occupancy","Safe rooms used for general storage that could impede rapid sheltering","Staff and students unaware of safe room locations or activation criteria"]
common_failures: ["Misunderstanding of building code-required rooms versus engineered safe rooms","Assuming interior classroom or office equals safe room without engineering review","Improvised or ad hoc “safe room” designations during incidents","No maintenance or inspection program for doors, frames, and anchorage"]
assessment_questions: ["Do you have any engineered or rated safe rooms and what are they designed for (weather, blast, forced entry)?","Where are these rooms located and how do occupants reach them?","How are doors, frames, and hardware maintained and tested?","What are the occupancy limits and how are they communicated?","How do you coordinate safe room use with local responders and emergency plans?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Confirm which rooms, if any, meet recognized safe room standards for the intended hazard.","Keep safe rooms accessible, free of clutter, and clearly marked.","Test doors and locking mechanisms regularly under realistic conditions.","Provide safe rooms with basic life-safety provisions (lighting, communications, ventilation).","Document safe room design and integrate into emergency instructions and drills."]
standards_references: ["FEMA P-361 and related safe room design standards","ICC 500 – storm shelter design criteria","CISA guidance for protective spaces within facilities"]
psa_notes: A sign on a door does not make a safe room. PSAs should verify the existence of actual structural and hardware features appropriate to the claimed hazard.

name: Clear Zones
overview: Clear zones are buffer areas along perimeters kept free of vegetation, structures, and obstructions to improve visibility, enable detection, and reduce concealment or climbing opportunities.
indicators_of_risk: ["Vegetation, dumpsters, or equipment placed directly against fences or walls","Perimeter lighting unable to penetrate dense growth","Obstructions providing footholds or concealment for intruders","Lack of defined interior and exterior clear zones around fences","No maintenance schedule to control vegetation height"]
common_failures: ["Allowing landscaping to encroach gradually on security boundaries","Placing storage or parked vehicles near perimeter lines","Failing to coordinate facilities, groundskeeping, and security","Ignoring exterior-side clear zones, focusing only inside the fence"]
assessment_questions: ["How wide are your interior and exterior clear zones?","Is vegetation controlled to maintain full visibility of the fence line?","Are maintenance crews aware of security clear zone requirements?","Do cameras and lighting have unobstructed views along the perimeter?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Maintain a minimum recommended clear zone on both sides of perimeter fencing.","Remove objects that provide concealment or climbing assistance.","Coordinate security requirements with landscaping and groundskeeping teams.","Ensure lighting and cameras can effectively view through the clear zone."]
standards_references: ["CISA perimeter security guidance","CPTED principles regarding visibility"]
psa_notes: Walk both sides of the fence. The exterior side is often neglected and reveals the real vulnerability.

name: Response Procedures
overview: Response procedures define the actions staff take when an emergency occurs. They translate training and policy into rapid behaviors that save lives.
indicators_of_risk: ["Staff unsure whether to evacuate, lockdown, or shelter for common scenarios","No clear escalation path for reporting incidents or suspicious activity","Procedures rely on single individuals who may not be present at the time of an incident","Contradictory procedures between departments, shifts, or buildings","Procedures untested in drills, leading to confusion under stress"]
common_failures: ["Plans written by committees that do not reflect operational realities","Procedures too complex to execute quickly in high-stress scenarios","Ambiguous language (“secure the area”) without actionable instructions","Failure to integrate communications and notification tools"]
assessment_questions: ["How do staff know which protective action to take during an emergency?","What procedures exist for reporting and escalating suspicious activity?","Are there clear, role-based responsibilities for each type of incident?","How do drills and exercises feed into procedure refinement?","What procedures exist for after-hours or low-staffing conditions?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Develop clear protective action procedures (evacuate, lockdown, shelter).","Use plain language and remove ambiguity from instructions.","Ensure procedures are role-based and not dependent on a single individual.","Integrate procedures with notification systems, access control, and VSS.","Regularly test procedures through drills and update based on lessons learned."]
standards_references: ["CISA Protective Actions guidance","NFPA 1600 – emergency management and business continuity principles","FEMA emergency operations planning guidance"]
psa_notes: Interviewing a random staff member reveals the true state of response readiness. If answers vary widely, procedures are not institutionalized.

name: Alarm Panels
overview: Alarm panels are the central controllers for intrusion detection devices such as door contacts, motion sensors, glass-break detectors, and panic buttons. They manage arming states, zones, communication paths, and event logging.
indicators_of_risk: ["Alarm panels located in unsecured, publicly accessible, or easily reached areas","Panel labels, zone lists, or programming undocumented or out of date","Frequent “trouble” conditions (low battery, line fault) left unresolved","Single communication path with no redundancy (e.g., only POTS line)","Staff unsure how to arm/disarm or interpret panel messages"]
common_failures: ["Treating alarm panels as a black box maintained only by vendors","Allowing codes or credentials to be widely shared among staff","Failing to update zones and descriptions after building changes","No regular testing of communication paths to monitoring centers","Leaving default installer codes active or poorly controlled"]
assessment_questions: ["Where are alarm panels located and how are they physically protected?","Who controls panel programming, codes, and zone configuration?","How are alarm, trouble, and supervisory conditions differentiated for responders?","What communication paths (cellular, IP, radio, phone) are used and how are they tested?","Is there a current, accurate zone list mapped to floor plans?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Relocate or secure alarm panels in controlled, access-restricted rooms.","Document and maintain up-to-date zone lists and panel programming records.","Implement strong code management practices with unique, role-based credentials.","Test alarm communication paths regularly with the monitoring entity.","Remove default/installer codes and restrict programming access to authorized personnel."]
standards_references: ["UL 681 – Installation and classification of central-station alarm systems","NFPA 730 – Guide for Premises Security (intrusion and alarm systems)","CISA facility intrusion detection guidance"]
psa_notes: A sophisticated sensor layout is meaningless if the panel is poorly located, mis-programmed, or communicating over a single fragile path. PSAs should ask to see the panel, not just the keypad.

name: Natural Surveillance
overview: Natural surveillance uses design and layout to maximize visibility of people, entrances, and critical areas. It reduces hiding places and increases the likelihood that suspicious behavior will be noticed and challenged.
indicators_of_risk: ["Bushes, walls, or structures blocking sightlines to entrances or gathering areas","Hidden recesses or alcoves near doors, loading docks, or paths","Parking areas not visible from occupied spaces or patrol routes","Windows covered or tinted such that staff cannot see outside activity","Blind internal corridors with no line of sight to staff or monitoring"]
common_failures: ["Landscaping installed for aesthetics without considering security sightlines","Add-on structures or temporary storage blocking originally clear views","Security cameras used as a substitute for basic visibility","Failure to reassess sightlines after renovations"]
assessment_questions: ["From where can staff see the main entrances and approaches?","Have you evaluated landscaping and structures for hiding places or ambush points?","Are parking and pedestrian routes visible from occupied areas or patrols?","Do interior layouts allow informal observation of hallways and common spaces?","Have sightlines been reassessed since major renovations or additions?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Trim or remove vegetation and obstacles that block views of key areas.","Design circulation so high-traffic areas are observable by staff or security.","Use windows and open layouts to enable passive monitoring where appropriate.","Position formal surveillance (cameras, posts) to complement natural surveillance."]
standards_references: ["CPTED foundational principles","CPTED guidance from law enforcement or CISA for specific sectors"]
psa_notes: PSAs should literally walk the site with “offender eyes”: where could someone approach unseen, wait unseen, or work unseen? That is your natural surveillance gap map.

name: Gates
overview: Security gates regulate authorized vehicle and pedestrian entry at controlled perimeter points. Their effectiveness depends on structural integrity, locking mechanisms, monitoring, and integration with access control and guard procedures.
indicators_of_risk: ["Gates routinely left open for convenience during operational hours","No access control—gates opened manually without verification","Gate motors, hinges, or rollers in poor condition or easily bypassed","No camera coverage or lighting for gate operations","Tailgating or piggybacking common at gate entry points"]
common_failures: ["Treating gates as traffic-control devices instead of security controls","Not integrating gates with access systems, cameras, or intercoms","Failing to maintain gate operators, resulting in frequent malfunctions","Allowing manual override procedures to become the “normal” state","No contingency plan for gate failure during emergencies"]
assessment_questions: ["How do vehicles and pedestrians request access through this gate?","Is there a verification step before opening?","Are gates monitored by cameras or sensors?","Are gate operators inspected and maintained routinely?","What is the procedure if the gate becomes inoperable?"]
mitigation_guidance: ["Integrate gates with access control, intercom, and camera systems.","Implement anti-tailgating procedures and technology where feasible.","Ensure gate hardware receives regular preventive maintenance.","Provide adequate lighting for nighttime gate operations.","Establish emergency manual operation procedures and training."]
standards_references: ["CISA perimeter security guidelines","DoD UFC standards for controlled access points (adapted as appropriate)"]
psa_notes: Gates are often left open for convenience—observe real-world behavior, not stated policy.
